1.o: The lessons here given are plain, and likely to benefit those who feel their own ignorance, and their need to be taught. If young people take heed to their ways, according to Solomon's Proverbs, they will gain knowledge and discretion. Solomon speaks of the most important points of truth, and a greater than Solomon is here. Christ speaks by his word and by his Spirit. Christ is the Word and the Wisdom of God, and he is made to us wisdom.
1.m:
These lessons are clear and helpful for anyone who knows they don't know everything and want to learn more. If young people pay attention to what Solomon teaches in his proverbs, they will gain knowledge and good judgment. 

Solomon talks about the most important truths. But someone even greater than Solomon is speaking now—Christ himself. Through his words and his Spirit, Christ gives us wisdom. He is the very wisdom and word of God, becoming our wisdom too.

1.o: Fools are persons who have no true wisdom, who follow their own devices, without regard to reason, or reverence for God. Children are reasonable creatures, and when we tell them what they must do, we must tell them why. But they are corrupt and wilful, therefore with the instruction there is need of a law. Let Divine truths and commands be to us most honourable; let us value them, and then they shall be so to us.
1.m:
Fools are people who lack real wisdom. They act on their own ideas without thinking or respecting God. 

Children are smart beings. When we tell them what to do, we should explain why too. But children often don’t listen well, so rules are needed along with guidance.

We should honor God’s teachings and commands by valuing them highly. Then they will truly matter to us.

1.o: Wicked people are zealous in seducing others into the paths of the destroyer: sinners love company in sin. But they have so much the more to answer for. How cautious young people should be! “Consent thou not.” Do not say as they say, nor do as they do, or would have thee to do; have no fellowship with them. Who could think that it should be a pleasure to one man to destroy another! See their idea of worldly wealth; but it is neither substance, nor precious. It is the ruinous mistake of thousands, that they overvalue the wealth of this world. Men promise themselves in vain that sin will turn to their advantage. The way of sin is down-hill; men cannot stop themselves. Would young people shun temporal and eternal ruin, let them refuse to take one step in these destructive paths. Men's greediness of gain hurries them upon practices which will not suffer them or others to live out half their days. What is a man profited, though he gain the world, if he lose his life? much less if he lose his soul?
1.m:
Bad people try hard to get others to join them in doing wrong things. Sinners enjoy having friends who help them sin. They will have to face even greater judgment because of this. Young people need to be very careful. Don’t agree with them. Don’t talk like they do or follow what they do. Stay away from them.

It’s shocking that someone would find joy in hurting others. Look at how they see money. To them, it seems valuable, but it isn’t really worth anything important. Many people make the big mistake of thinking money is all that matters. People fool themselves by believing sin will bring them good things. Once you start sinning, it’s hard to stop.

If young people want to avoid both earthly and spiritual harm, they shouldn’t take even one step toward those harmful ways. Greed often leads people to do dangerous things that shorten their lives. 

What use is it for someone to gain everything in the world if they lose their real self-worth and their soul? It’s even worse to lose their soul.

1.o: Solomon, having showed how dangerous it is to hearken to the temptations of Satan, here declares how dangerous it is not to hearken to the calls of God. Christ himself is Wisdom, is Wisdoms. Three sorts of persons are here called by Him: 1. Simple ones. Sinners are fond of their simple notions of good and evil, their simple prejudices against the ways of God, and flatter themselves in their wickedness. 2. Scorners. Proud, jovial people, that make a jest of every thing. Scoffers at religion, that run down every thing sacred and serious. 3. Fools. Those are the worst of fools that hate to be taught, and have a rooted dislike to serious godliness. The precept is plain; Turn you at my reproof. We do not make a right use of reproofs, if we do not turn from evil to that which is good. The promises are very encouraging. Men cannot turn by any power of their own; but God answers, Behold, I will pour out my Spirit unto you. Special grace is needful to sincere conversion. But that grace shall never be denied to any who seek it. The love of Christ, and the promises mingled with his reproofs, surely should have the attention of every one. It may well be asked, how long men mean to proceed in such a perilous path, when the uncertainty of life and the consequences of dying without Christ are considered? Now sinners live at ease, and set sorrow at defiance; but their calamity will come. Now God is ready to hear their prayers; but then they shall cry in vain. Are we yet despisers of wisdom? Let us hearken diligently, and obey the Lord Jesus, that we may enjoy peace of conscience and confidence in God; be free from evil, in life, in death, and for ever.
1.m:
Solomon explains that it’s just as dangerous to ignore God’s call as it is to listen to Satan’s temptations. Here are three types of people God calls out:

1. Simple people. These are folks who stick to their basic ideas about what’s right and wrong, even if those ideas aren’t true. They think highly of themselves despite being sinful.
   
2. Scorners. People who mock everything, especially religious things. They laugh at anything serious or sacred.

3. Fools. The biggest fools don’t want to learn and really dislike being serious about God.

The command is clear: Change your ways after hearing my correction. If we don’t turn away from bad behavior toward better actions, we’re not using corrections properly. 

God makes some great promises too. He says, “I’ll give you my Spirit.” True conversion needs special help from God. But anyone who seeks it won’t be turned away. 

Christ’s love and the hope in his words should get everyone’s attention. How much longer will people keep going down a risky path? Life is uncertain, and there are terrible consequences for dying without knowing Christ. Right now, sinners might feel comfortable and avoid trouble, but their problems will eventually catch up to them. 

Right now, God listens to their prayers, but later on, they’ll cry out in vain. Are we still ignoring God’s wisdom? Let’s pay close attention and follow Jesus so we can have peace in our hearts and trust in God. This way, we’ll stay free from evil, both while alive and forever after.

2.o: Those who earnestly seek heavenly wisdom, will never complain that they have lost their labour; and the freeness of the gift does not do away the necessity of our diligence, Joh 6:27 Let them seek, and they shall find it; let them ask, and it shall be given them. Observe who are thus favoured. They are the righteous, on whom the image of God is renewed, which consists in righteousness. If we depend upon God, and seek to him for wisdom, he will enable us to keep the paths of judgment.
2.m:
People who truly want to gain wisdom from heaven won’t ever feel like they wasted their time trying. Even though God freely gives this wisdom, we still need to work hard for it. John 6:27 tells us to look for it, and we’ll find it if we do. 

Notice who gets this special favor. It’s people who are righteous—those whose hearts reflect God’s goodness. When we trust God and ask him for wisdom, he helps us stay on the right path.

2.o: If we are truly wise, we shall be careful to avoid all evil company and evil practices. When wisdom has dominion over us, then it not only fills the head, but enters into the heart, and will preserve, both against corruptions within and temptations without. The ways of sin are ways of darkness, uncomfortable and unsafe: what fools are those who leave the plain, pleasant, lightsome paths of uprightness, to walk in such ways! They take pleasure in sin; both in committing it, and in seeing others commit it. Every wise man will shun such company. True wisdom will also preserve from those who lead to fleshly lusts, which defile the body, that living temple, and war against the soul. These are evils which excite the sorrow of every serious mind, and cause every reflecting parent to look upon his children with anxiety, lest they should be entangled in such fatal snares. Let the sufferings of others be our warnings. Our Lord Jesus deters from sinful pleasures, by the everlasting torments which follow them. It is very rare that any who are caught in this snare of the devil, recover themselves; so much is the heart hardened, and the mind blinded, by the deceitfulness of this sin. Many think that this caution, besides the literal sense, is to be understood as a caution against idolatry, and subjecting the soul to the body, by seeking any forbidden object. The righteous must leave the earth as well as the wicked; but the earth is a very different thing to them. To the wicked it is all the heaven they ever shall have; to the righteous it is the place of preparation for heaven. And is it all one to us, whether we share with the wicked in the miseries of their latter end, or share those everlasting joys that shall crown believers?
2.m:
If we want to be wise, we need to stay away from bad people and bad habits. Wisdom helps us guard against wrong thoughts inside us and resist outside temptations too. Sinful ways are dark and dangerous. Why would anyone choose to walk on rough, unpleasant roads instead of smooth, bright ones? People who enjoy sinning find joy in doing it themselves and watching others do it too. Wise people avoid these kinds of friends. 

True wisdom keeps us safe from those who try to pull us toward harmful desires that ruin our bodies and souls. This kind of behavior worries thoughtful parents because they fear their kids might get trapped in these deadly traps. We should learn from the mistakes of others. Jesus warns about eternal punishment that comes after sinful pleasures. Once someone gets stuck in the trap of sin, it’s hard to escape. Their hearts become stubborn and minds blind to the truth.

Some believe this advice also means avoiding idol worship and letting physical desires control the spirit. Both good and bad people die, but the earth means different things to each. For the wicked, it’s their only heaven. For the righteous, it’s just a stepping stone to true heaven. Does it matter if we end up suffering with the wicked or enjoying eternal happiness with believers?

3.o: In the way of believing obedience to God's commandments health and peace may commonly be enjoyed; and though our days may not be long upon earth, we shall live for ever in heaven. Let not mercy and truth forsake thee; God's mercy in promising, and his truth in performing: live up to them, keep up thine interest in them, and take the comfort of them. We must trust in the Lord with all our hearts, believing he is able and wise to do what is best. Those who know themselves, find their own understandings a broken reed, which, if they lean upon, will fail. Do not design any thing but what is lawful, and beg God to direct thee in every case, though it may seem quite plain. In all our ways that prove pleasant, in which we gain our point, we must acknowledge God with thankfulness. In all our ways that prove uncomfortable, and that are hedged up with thorns, we must acknowledge him with submission. It is promised, He shall direct thy paths; so that thy way shall be safe and good, and happy at last.
3.m:
Believing and obeying God’s commands usually brings health and peace. Even if our time on Earth isn’t long, we’ll have eternal life in heaven. Don't let God’s mercy and truth leave you. Trust in God’s promises and rely on His faithfulness. Live according to those promises and enjoy the blessings they bring.

We should put full trust in the Lord, knowing He has the power and wisdom to do what is best. People often realize their own minds are weak and unreliable. So don’t plan anything unless it’s right, and ask God to guide you even when things seem clear.

When things go well, thank God for helping you succeed. When things are hard, still submit to Him. God will lead your path safely and make sure everything turns out well in the end.

3.o: There is not a greater enemy to the fear of the Lord in the heart, than self-conceit of our own wisdom. The prudence and sobriety which religion teaches, tend not only to the health of the soul, but to the health of the body. Worldly wealth is but poor substance, yet, such as it is, we must honour God with it; and those that do good with what they have, shall have more to do more good with. Should the Lord visit us with trials and sickness, let us not forget that the exhortation speaks to us as to children, for our good. We must not faint under an affliction, be it ever so heavy and long, not be driven to despair, or use wrong means for relief. The father corrects the son whom he loves, because he loves him, and desires that he may be wise and good. Afflictions are so far from doing God's children any hurt, that, by the grace of God, they promote their holiness.
3.m:
Self-conceit is a big enemy to fearing the Lord. Religious teachings about being smart and careful help both our souls and bodies stay healthy. Even though worldly wealth isn't much, we should honor God with it. People who use what they have well will get more to use even better.

If the Lord sends us troubles or sickness, remember He’s telling us like a parent tells a child—out of love. Don’t give up during hard times, no matter how bad or long they are. Don’t lose hope or take wrong actions to feel better.

Parents punish their kids out of love, wanting them to grow wise and good. For God’s children, troubles don’t harm them. Instead, through His grace, these struggles help them become more holy.

3.o: No precious jewels or earthly treasures are worthy to be compared with true wisdom, whether the concerns of time or eternity be considered. We must make wisdom our business; we must venture all in it, and be willing to part with all for it. This Wisdom is the Lord Jesus Christ and his salvation, sought and obtained by faith and prayer. Were it not for unbelief, remaining sinfulness, and carelessness, we should find all our ways pleasantness, and our paths peace, for his are so; but we too often step aside from them, to our own hurt and grief. Christ is that Wisdom, by whom the worlds were made, and still are in being; happy are those to whom he is made of God wisdom. He has wherewithal to make good all his promises.
3.m:
True wisdom is more valuable than any treasure or jewel, whether we think about things here on earth or what happens after we die. We need to focus on gaining wisdom and be ready to give up everything for it. 

This wisdom means knowing Jesus Christ and finding salvation through faith and prayer. Without doubt, if we weren't lazy or sinful, we would always have joy and peace because following Jesus brings these blessings. But often, we wander off course and miss out on happiness.

Jesus created the world and keeps it going. People who know him receive wisdom from God. He can fulfill all his promises.

3.o: Let us not suffer Christ's words to depart from us, but keep sound wisdom and discretion; then shall we walk safely in his ways. The natural life, and all that belongs to it, shall be under the protection of God's providence; the spiritual life, and all its interests, under the protection of his grace, so that we shall be kept from falling into sin or trouble.
3.m:
Don't let Christ's teachings slip away from you. Hold onto good sense and careful thinking. Then you will walk safely on his path.

God protects our physical lives and everything connected to them. He also guards our spiritual lives and all their needs through his grace. This keeps us safe from sin and troubles.

3.o: Our business is to observe the precepts of Christ, and to copy his example; to do justice, to love mercy, and to beware of covetousness; to be ready for every good work, avoiding needless strife, and bearing evils, if possible, rather than seeking redress by law. It will be found there is little got by striving. Let us not envy prosperous oppressors; far be it from the disciples of Christ to choose any of their ways. These truths may be despised by the covetous and luxurious, but everlasting contempt will be the portion of such scorners, while Divine favour is shown to the humble believer.
3.m:
Our job is to follow Jesus' rules and set an example for others. We should do what’s right, show kindness, and avoid being greedy. We need to help whenever we can, stay out of pointless arguments, and take abuse instead of going to court. Usually, arguing doesn’t get you much. 

We shouldn’t wish we were rich like mean people who hurt others. As followers of Jesus, we don't want anything to do with those kinds of ways. People who ignore these teachings might think they’re smart, but God won’t honor them. Instead, God blesses those who are humble and trust him.

4.o: We must look upon our teachers as our fathers: though instruction carry in it reproof and correction, bid it welcome. Solomon's parents loved him, therefore taught him. Wise and godly men, in every age of the world, and rank in society, agree that true wisdom consists in obedience, and is united to happiness. Get wisdom, take pains for it. Get the rule over thy corruptions; take more pains to get this than the wealth of this world. An interest in Christ's salvation is necessary. This wisdom is the one thing needful. A soul without true wisdom and grace is a dead soul. How poor, contemptible, and wretched are those, who, with all their wealth and power, die without getting understanding, without Christ, without hope, and without God! Let us give heed to the sayings of Him who has the words of eternal life. Thus our path will be plain before us: by taking, and keeping fast hold of instruction, we shall avoid being straitened or stumbling.
4.m: We should see our teachers as our fathers. Even if they correct us, we should welcome their guidance. Solomon’s parents loved him, so they taught him. Throughout history, wise and godly people have agreed that real wisdom comes from obeying God, which leads to happiness. Work hard to gain wisdom. Try harder to control your bad habits than to earn money. Having an interest in Jesus' salvation is important. True wisdom and grace are what really matter. Without them, a person’s soul is dead. Those who have lots of money and power but don’t find true wisdom and faith are very sad. They die without knowing Christ, hope, or God. Listen carefully to the teachings of Jesus, who gives eternal life. By following his instructions, we won't stumble or face difficulties.

4.o: The way of evil men may seem pleasant, and the nearest way to compass some end; but it is an evil way, and will end ill; if thou love thy God and thy soul, avoid it. It is not said, Keep at a due distance, but at a great distance; never think you can get far enough from it. The way of the righteous is light; Christ is their Way, and he is the Light. The saints will not be perfect till they reach heaven, but there they shall shine as the sun in his strength. The way of sin is as darkness. The way of the wicked is dark, therefore dangerous; they fall into sin, but know not how to avoid it. They fall into trouble, but never seek to know wherefore God contends with them, nor what will be in the end of it. This is the way we are bid to shun. Attentive hearing the word of God, is a good sign of a work of grace begun in the heart, and a good means of carrying it on. There is in the word of God a proper remedy for all diseases of the soul. Keep thy heart with all diligence. We must set a strict guard upon our souls; keep our hearts from doing hurt, and getting hurt. A good reason is given; because out of it are the issues of life. Above all, we should seek from the Lord Jesus that living water, the sanctifying Spirit, issuing forth unto everlasting life. Thus we shall be enabled to put away a froward mouth and perverse lips; our eyes will be turned from beholding vanity, looking straight forward, and walking by the rule of God's word, treading in the steps of our Lord and Master. Lord, forgive the past, and enable us to follow thee more closely for the time to come.
4.m:
The path of bad people might look nice and quick to achieve goals, but it’s wrong and leads to trouble. If you love God and yourself, stay away from it. Don’t just keep a safe distance—stay very far from it. You can never be too careful. 

The path of good people is bright. Jesus is their guide and he gives them light. Saints won't be perfect until they reach heaven, but then they'll shine brightly. Sinners walk in darkness. Their path is dark and dangerous. They stumble into sins without knowing how to stop. They face troubles but don’t try to understand why or what will happen next. That’s the path to avoid.

Listening carefully to God’s Word shows that God is working in someone’s heart and helps grow faith. God’s Word has a cure for every sickness of the soul. Guard your heart carefully. Watch over your soul so it doesn’t do harm or suffer harm. 

There’s a good reason for this care. Everything important about life comes from your heart. Ask Jesus for the Holy Spirit, which brings eternal life. Then you’ll be able to control your tongue and lips. Your eyes will focus only on truth, following God’s rules and walking in Jesus’ footsteps.

Lord, forgive our past mistakes and help us follow you even closer in the future.

5.o: Solomon cautions all young men, as his children, to abstain from fleshly lusts. Some, by the adulterous woman, here understand idolatry, false doctrine, which tends to lead astray men's minds and manners; but the direct view is to warn against seventh-commandment sins. Often these have been, and still are, Satan's method of drawing men from the worship of God into false religion. Consider how fatal the consequences; how bitter the fruit! Take it any way, it wounds. It leads to the torments of hell. The direct tendency of this sin is to the destruction of body and soul. We must carefully avoid every thing which may be a step towards it. Those who would be kept from harm, must keep out of harm's way. If we thrust ourselves into temptation we mock God when we pray, Lead us not into temptation. How many mischiefs attend this sin! It blasts the reputation; it wastes time; it ruins the estate; it is destructive to health; it will fill the mind with horror. Though thou art merry now, yet sooner or later it will bring sorrow. The convinced sinner reproaches himself, and makes no excuse for his folly. By the frequent acts of sin, the habits of it become rooted and confirmed. By a miracle of mercy true repentance may prevent the dreadful consequences of such sins; but this is not often; far more die as they have lived. What can express the case of the self-ruined sinner in the eternal world, enduring the remorse of his conscience!
5.m:
Solomon warns young people to stay away from sexual desires. Some see the sinful woman mentioned here as representing false beliefs that lead people away from true faith. But Solomon’s main goal is to warn about breaking the commandment against adultery. 

This kind of sin has always led people away from worshipping God and into false religions. Think about how harmful the results can be—how bitter the outcome! This sin hurts you no matter what form it takes. It can destroy both your body and soul. To protect yourself, avoid anything that might lead you down this path. If you put yourself in tempting situations, you’re mocking God when you ask Him not to let you be tempted.

How much damage does this sin cause? It ruins your good name, wastes your time, destroys your wealth, harms your health, and fills your mind with fear. Even if you feel happy now, eventually it will bring sadness. A person who realizes their mistake feels ashamed and doesn’t try to excuse themselves.

The more someone sins, the stronger those bad habits become. Sometimes, through God’s great mercy, true regret can save a person from the worst outcomes of their sins. But usually, people end up facing the full consequences of their actions. Can words describe the suffering of someone who destroyed themselves with sin, feeling endless guilt in the afterlife?

5.o: Lawful marriage is a means God has appointed to keep from these destructive vices. But we are not properly united, except as we attend to God's word, seeking his direction and blessing, and acting with affection. Ever remember, that though secret sins may escape the eyes of our fellow-creatures, yet a man's ways are before the eyes of the Lord, who not only sees, but ponders all his goings. Those who are so foolish as to choose the way of sin, are justly left of God to themselves, to go on in the way to destruction.
5.m: Marriage is a way God set up to stop harmful behaviors. But we aren't truly joined together unless we listen to God’s word, seek his guidance and blessings, and act with love. Remember, even if others don’t see our hidden sins, God sees everything. He watches us closely and considers all our actions. People who choose to do wrong are left by God to follow paths that lead to destruction.

6.o: If we live as directed by the word of God, we shall find it profitable even in this present world. We are stewards of our worldly substance, and have to answer to the Lord for our disposal of it; to waste it in rash schemes, or such plans as may entangle us in difficulties and temptations, is wrong. A man ought never to be surety for more than he is able and willing to pay, and can afford to pay, without wronging his family; he ought to look upon every sum he is engaged for, as his own debt. If we must take all this care to get our debts to men forgiven, much more to obtain forgiveness with God. Humble thyself to him, make sure of Christ as thy Friend, to plead for thee; pray earnestly that thy sins may be pardoned, and that thou mayest be kept from going down to the pit.
6.m:
If we follow what the Bible tells us, we will find it helpful even here on Earth. We are responsible for how we use our money and possessions, and we have to give an account to God about them. It’s wrong to spend our resources on risky ventures that might cause trouble or lead us into sin. 

We should only agree to pay back loans if we really can and want to do so without hurting our families. When we promise to repay someone else’s debt, we need to see it as our own obligation.

Since we need to be careful about getting out of debt to people, we should be even more diligent about being forgiven by God. Lower yourself before Him, trust Jesus as your friend who pleads for you. Pray sincerely for your sins to be forgiven and ask to be protected from falling into deeper trouble.

6.o: Diligence in business is every man's wisdom and duty; not so much that he may attain worldly wealth, as that he may not be a burden to others, or a scandal to the church. The ants are more diligent than slothful men. We may learn wisdom from the meanest insects, and be shamed by them. Habits of indolence and indulgence grow upon people. Thus life runs to waste; and poverty, though at first at a distance, gradually draws near, like a traveller; and when it arrives, is like an armed man, too strong to be resisted. All this may be applied to the concerns of our souls. How many love their sleep of sin, and their dreams of worldly happiness! Shall we not seek to awaken such? Shall we not give diligence to secure our own salvation?
6.m:
Working hard at your job is smart and what everyone should do. It’s not just about getting rich, but making sure you don’t become a burden on others or bring shame to the church. 

Ants work harder than lazy people. We can learn from even the smallest bugs and feel embarrassed if we’re less productive.

People who get used to being lazy often stay that way. This leads to wasting time, and then poverty slowly creeps up, like a traveler coming closer. When poverty hits, it’s like facing a powerful enemy you can't beat.

We can apply these lessons to our spiritual lives too. Many people love sleeping through sin and dreaming about worldly pleasures. Shouldn’t we try to wake them up? And shouldn’t we work hard to save ourselves spiritually?

6.o: If the slothful are to be condemned, who do nothing, much more those that do all the ill they can. Observe how such a man is described. He says and does every thing artfully, and with design. His ruin shall come without warning, and without relief. Here is a list of things hateful to God. Those sins are in a special manner provoking to God, which are hurtful to the comfort of human life. These things which God hates, we must hate in ourselves; it is nothing to hate them in others. Let us shun all such practices, and watch and pray against them; and avoid, with marked disapproval, all who are guilty of them, whatever may be their rank.
6.m:
If lazy people deserve punishment for doing nothing, even more so should those who do only bad things be punished. Notice how such a person acts. They always speak and act carefully, with intent. Their downfall will happen suddenly, with no help coming. 

Here is a list of things that God finds hateful. Sins that harm people’s happiness especially anger God. We must hate these sins in ourselves, not just in others. Let’s stay away from such actions and pray against them. Also, let’s avoid associating with anyone who commits these sins, no matter their status.

6.o: The word of God has something to say to us upon all occasions. Let not faithful reproofs ever make us uneasy. When we consider how much this sin abounds, how heinous adultery is in its own nature, of what evil consequence it is, and how certainly it destroys the spiritual life in the soul, we shall not wonder that the cautions against it are so often repeated. Let us notice the subjects of this chapter. Let us remember Him who willingly became our Surety, when we were strangers and enemies. And shall Christians, who have such prospects, motives, and examples, be slothful and careless? Shall we neglect what is pleasing to God, and what he will graciously reward? May we closely watch every sense by which poison can enter our minds or affections.
6.m:
The word of God has something helpful to say about every situation. Faithful warnings should not upset us. 

When we think about how common this sin is, how terrible adultery really is, and how it ruins the spiritual life inside us, we won't be surprised that warnings against it come up again and again.

Let’s pay attention to the topics covered in this chapter. Remember Jesus, who freely offered himself for us even though we were strangers and enemies.

With these hopes, reasons, and examples before us, shouldn’t Christians be eager and careful? Shouldn’t we do things that please God and earn his rewards?

We need to guard ourselves carefully from anything harmful that could affect our thoughts or feelings.

7.o: We must lay up God's commandments safely. Not only, Keep them, and you shall live; but, Keep them as those that cannot live without them. Those that blame strict and careful walking as needless and too precise, consider not that the law is to be kept as the apple of the eye; indeed the law in the heart is the eye of the soul. Let the word of God dwell in us, and so be written where it will be always at hand to be read. Thus we shall be kept from the fatal effects of our own passions, and the snares of Satan. Let God's word confirm our dread of sin, and resolutions against it.
7.m: We need to keep God’s commands safe. It’s not just about keeping them to live, but keeping them because we can’t live without them. People who think being very careful about following rules is unnecessary should remember that the law needs to be protected carefully, like an apple of the eye. The Word of God should live inside us, ready for us to read anytime. This way, we’ll avoid harmful results from our emotions and stay away from Satan’s traps. Let God’s word strengthen our fear of sin and help us decide against doing wrong things.

7.o: Here is an affecting example of the danger of youthful lusts. It is a history or a parable of the most instructive kind. Will any one dare to venture on temptations that lead to impurity, after Solomon has set before his eyes in so lively and plain a manner, the danger of even going near them? Then is he as the man who would dance on the edge of a lofty rock, when he has just seen another fall headlong from the same place. The misery of self-ruined sinners began in disregard to God's blessed commands. We ought daily to pray that we may be kept from running into temptation, else we invite the enemies of our souls to spread snares for us. Ever avoid the neighbourhood of vice. Beware of sins which are said to be pleasant sins. They are the more dangerous, because they most easily gain the heart, and close it against repentance. Do nothing till thou hast well considered the end of it. Were a man to live as long as Methuselah, and to spend all his days in the highest delights sin can offer, one hour of the anguish and tribulation that must follow, would far outweigh them.
7.m: This story shows how dangerous young desires can be. It’s a very helpful lesson. Would anyone want to risk being tempted toward immorality after seeing what happened to Solomon? That’s like dancing on the edge of a high cliff right after watching someone fall off. Sinners who ruin themselves often start by ignoring God’s good rules. We should ask God each day to keep us away from temptation, since giving in invites trouble. Stay away from places where bad things happen. Be careful of sins called “pleasant” ones. These are extra dangerous because they’re easier to fall for and harder to feel sorry about later. Think carefully before you do anything. Even if someone lived as long as Methuselah and spent their whole life enjoying sinful pleasures, one hour of the pain that comes afterward would still be worse than all those moments of pleasure.

8.o: The will of God is made known by the works of creation, and by the consciences of men, but more clearly by Moses and the prophets. The chief difficulty is to get men to attend to instruction. Yet attention to the words of Christ, will guide the most ignorant into saving knowledge of the truth. Where there is an understanding heart, and willingness to receive the truth in love, wisdom is valued above silver and gold.
8.m:
God shows his will through nature and people’s inner feelings, but he makes it clearer through Moses and the prophets. 

The main challenge is getting people to pay attention to teachings. But if someone listens to what Jesus says, even the least educated person can learn about true salvation.

When someone has a good mind and wants to accept the truth with love, then wisdom becomes more valuable than money.

8.o: Wisdom, here is Christ, in whom are all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge; it is Christ in the word, and Christ in the heart; not only Christ revealed to us, but Christ revealed in us. All prudence and skill are from the Lord. Through the redemption of Christ's precious blood, the riches of his grace abound in all wisdom and prudence. Man found out many inventions for ruin; God found one for our recovery. He hates pride and arrogance, evil ways and froward conversation; these render men unwilling to hear his humbling, awakening, holy instructions. True religion gives men the best counsel in all difficult cases, and helps to make their way plain. His wisdom makes all truly happy who receive it in the love of Christ Jesus. Seek him early, seek him earnestly, seek him before any thing else. Christ never said, Seek in vain. Those who love Christ, are such as have seen his loveliness, and have had his love shed abroad in their hearts; therefore they are happy. They shall be happy in this world, or in that which is beyond compare better. Wealth gotten by vanity will soon be diminished, but that which is well got, will wear well; and that which is well spent upon works of piety and charity, will be lasting. If they have not riches and honour in this world, they shall have that which is infinitely better. They shall be happy in the grace of God. Christ, by his Spirit, guides believers into all truth, and so leads them in the way of righteousness; and they walk after the Spirit. Also, they shall be happy in the glory of God hereafter. In Wisdom's promises, believers have goods laid up, not for days and years, but for eternity; her fruit therefore is better than gold.
8.m:
Here is Christ, full of all wisdom and knowledge. It’s Christ in the Word and Christ in our hearts—not just known by us, but living within us. All good judgment and skill come from the Lord. Because of Christ’s sacrifice, God’s grace fills us with wisdom and understanding. 

People create many things that cause harm, but God gave us one way to heal. He doesn’t like pride, arrogance, bad behavior, or stubbornness. These things make people resistant to hearing his humble, helpful teachings. 

True faith offers the best advice in tough situations and clears the path ahead. When we accept God’s wisdom with love for Christ, it brings true happiness. We should look for him first thing in the morning and keep looking hard. Christ always answers those who search sincerely. People who love Christ see his beauty and feel his love, making them joyful. 

They’ll find joy now or even more joy later. Money gained through lies won't last long, but money earned honestly and used wisely on good deeds lasts forever. Even if they don’t get rich or famous here, they'll gain far greater rewards. 

Their happiness comes from God’s favor. The Holy Spirit guides believers toward truth and righteousness. They follow the Spirit’s leading. And they will be happy with God’s glory in heaven. Believers store up eternal blessings, much better than earthly wealth. So, the results of following wisdom are better than gold.

8.o: The Son of God declares himself to have been engaged in the creation of the world. How able, how fit is the Son of God to be the Saviour of the world, who was the Creator of it! The Son of God was ordained, before the world, to that great work. Does he delight in saving wretched sinners, and shall not we delight in his salvation?
8.m: The Son of God says he helped create the world. How well qualified is the Son of God to save the world if he created it? The Son of God was chosen before the world began for this important task. He enjoys saving sinful people, so shouldn't we enjoy his salvation?

8.o: Surely we should hearken to Christ's voice with the readiness of children. Let us all be wise, and not refuse such mercy. Blessed are those who hear the Saviour's voice, and wait on him with daily reading, meditation, and prayer. The children of the world find time for vain amusements, without neglecting what they deem the one thing needful. Does it not show contempt of Wisdom's instructions, when people professing godliness, seek excuses for neglecting the means of grace? Christ is Wisdom, and he is Life to all believers; nor can we obtain God's favour, unless we find Christ, and are found in him. Those who offend Christ deceive themselves; sin is a wrong to the soul. Sinners die because they will die, which justifies God when he judges.
8.m:
We should listen to Christ’s voice like children do. Let’s all be smart and not turn down his kindness. It’s good for us to hear the Savior’s words and spend time each day reading, thinking about, and praying to him. People in the world find time for fun things without ignoring what they think is important. Isn’t it disrespectful to pretend you care about God while making excuses to skip church and prayer?

Christ is wisdom and life for everyone who believes in him. We cannot get God’s favor unless we find Christ and stay close to him. When people offend Christ, they fool themselves. Sin hurts our souls. Sinners face death because they choose to, so God is right to judge them.

9.o: Christ has prepared ordinances to which his people are admitted, and by which nourishment is given here to those that believe in him, as well as mansions in heaven hereafter. The ministers of the gospel go forth to invite the guests. The call is general, and shuts out none that do not shut out themselves. Our Saviour came, not to call the righteous, but sinners; not the wise in their own eyes, who say they see. We must keep from the company and foolish pleasures of the ungodly, or we never can enjoy the pleasures of a holy life. It is vain to seek the company of wicked men in the hope of doing them good; we are far more likely to be corrupted by them. It is not enough to forsake the foolish, we must join those that walk in wisdom. There is no true wisdom but in the way of religion, no true life but in the end of that way. Here is the happiness of those that embrace it. A man cannot be profitable to God; it is for our own good. Observe the shame and ruin of those who slight it. God is not the Author of sin: and Satan can only tempt, he cannot force. Thou shalt bear the loss of that which thou scornest: it will add to thy condemnation.
9.m:
Christ has set up ways for his followers to receive help and support both now and in heaven later on. Church leaders spread invitations to these special gatherings. Anyone can come if they don’t refuse.

Jesus came to save sinners, not self-righteous people. He calls those who admit they need help, not those who think they already know everything.

To live a holy life, we must avoid bad influences and sinful activities. Trying to help others by being around them often leads us to do wrong things too. Just staying away from evil isn't enough—we should choose to be with godly people instead.

True wisdom comes from following religious teachings. True joy comes from living a godly life. Those who accept Jesus find real happiness.

We can’t benefit God by serving him, but we greatly profit ourselves. Notice what happens to those who ignore God’s offer—they face shame and ruin.

God doesn’t cause anyone to sin. Satan can only tempt, not force. You’ll suffer because of the choices you make. Ignoring God’s invitation will only hurt you more.

9.o: How diligent the tempter is, to seduce unwary souls into sin! Carnal, sensual pleasure, stupifies conscience, and puts out the sparks of conviction. This tempter has no solid reason to offer; and where she gets dominion in a soul, all knowledge of holy things is lost and forgotten. She is very violent and pressing. We need to seek and pray for true wisdom, for Satan has many ways to withdraw our souls from Christ. Not only worldly lusts and abandoned seducers prove fatal to the souls of men; but false teachers, with doctrines that flatter pride and give liberty to lusts, destroy thousands. They especially draw off such as have received only partial serious impressions. The depths of Satan are depths of hell; and sin, without remorse, is ruin, ruin without remedy. Solomon shows the hook; those that believe him, will not meddle with the bait. Behold the wretched, empty, unsatisfying, deceitful, and stolen pleasure sin proposes; and may our souls be so desirous of the everlasting enjoyment of Christ, that on earth we may live to him, daily, by faith, and ere long be with him in glory.
9.m:
The devil works hard to trick people who aren't careful into sinning. Sensual pleasures confuse the mind and stop us from feeling guilty about wrongdoings. He offers no good reasons and takes over our minds, making us forget everything holy. He pushes really hard.

We should ask God for real wisdom because Satan uses many tricks to pull our hearts away from Jesus. It’s not just worldliness and bad influences that lead people to ruin. False teachers with ideas that feed pride and let sins run wild also cause harm. These teachers especially hurt those who only partly think seriously about God.

Satan's traps are deep pits leading to destruction. Sinning without regret leads to ruin with no way out. Solomon warns about the dangers of temptation. Those who listen to him won’t get caught by the trap.

Look at how empty and fake the pleasures of sin are. May we desire eternal joy with Christ so much that we live for him each day here on Earth and one day join him in heaven.

10.o: The comfort of parents much depends on their children; and this suggests to both, motives to their duties.
10.m: Parents find happiness in their children, which encourages both parents and children to do their duties.

10.o: Though the righteous may be poor, the Lord will not suffer him to want what is needful for spiritual life.
10.m: Even if the righteous person is poor, the Lord makes sure they don't lack what they need for spiritual life.

10.o: Those who are fervent in spirit, serving the Lord, are likely to be rich in faith, and rich in good works.
10.m: People who serve the Lord with great enthusiasm will probably have strong faith and do many good things.

10.o: Here is just blame of those who trifle away opportunities, both for here and for hereafter.
10.m: This passage points out the fault of people who waste chances, both for now and forever.

10.o: Abundance of blessings shall abide on good men; real blessings.
10.m: Good people will have many blessings in their lives; real blessings.

10.o: Both the just and the wicked must die; but between their souls there is a vast difference.
10.m: Both good people and bad people will die, but there is a big difference between their souls.

10.o: The wise in heart puts his knowledge in practice.
10.m: The smart person uses their knowledge.

10.o: Dissemblers, after all their shuffling, will be exposed.
10.m: People who hide the truth will eventually be uncovered.

10.o: Trick and artifice will be no excuse for iniquity.
10.m: Deception won't excuse wrong behavior.

10.o: The good man's mouth is always open to teach, comfort, and correct others.
10.m: A good person always talks to teach, comfort, and correct others.

10.o: Where there is hatred, every thing stirs up strife. By bearing with each other, peace and harmony are preserved.
10.m: Hatred causes arguments. By tolerating each other, we keep peace.

10.o: Those that foolishly go on in wicked ways, prepare rods for themselves.
10.m: People who keep doing bad things are preparing trouble for themselves.

10.o: Whatever knowledge may be useful, we must lay it up, that it may not be to seek when we want it. The wise gain this wisdom by reading, by hearing the word, by meditation, by prayer, by faith in Christ, who is made of God unto us wisdom.
10.m: We should save whatever knowledge might help us so we have it when we need it. Wise people get their understanding from reading, listening to the word, thinking about it, praying, and having faith in Christ, who gives us wisdom from God.

10.o: This refers to the common mistakes both of rich and poor, as to their outward condition. Rich people's wealth exposes them to many dangers; while a poor man may live comfortably, if he is content, keeps a good conscience, and lives by faith.
10.m: This talks about the mistakes both rich and poor people can make based on their situation. Wealthy people face many dangers because of their riches. A poor person can live well if they are happy with what they have, keep a clear conscience, and trust in God.

10.o: Perhaps a righteous man has no more than what he works hard for, but that labour tends to life.
10.m: Maybe a good person has only what they earn through hard work, but their effort leads to a better life.

10.o: The traveller that has missed his way, and cannot bear to be told of it, and to be shown the right way, must err still.
10.m: A person who is lost and doesn't want to admit it or follow directions will stay lost.

10.o: He is especially a fool who thinks to hide anything from God; and malice is no better.
10.m: It is especially foolish to try to hide anything from God. Malice is just as bad.

10.o: Those that speak much, speak much amiss. He that checks himself is a wise man, and therein consults his own peace.
10.m: People who talk a lot often say things they shouldn't. A smart person thinks before speaking and avoids trouble that way.

10.o: The tongue of the just is sincere, freed from the dross of guile and evil design. Pious discourse is spiritual food to the needy. Fools die for want of a heart, so the word is; for want of thought.
10.m: The honest person speaks truthfully, without deceit or bad intentions. Good conversations give spiritual nourishment to those who need it. Fools often suffer because they lack wisdom and careful thinking.

10.o: That wealth which is truly desirable, has no vexation of spirit in the enjoyment; no grief for the loss; no guilt by the abuse of it. What comes from the love of God, has the grace of God for its companion.
10.m: True wealth doesn’t cause worry when you have it, sadness when you lose it, or guilt if you misuse it. When it comes from God’s love, it brings God’s favor along with it.

10.o: Only foolish and wicked men divert themselves with doing harm to others, or tempting to sin.
10.m: Only foolish and wicked people amuse themselves by hurting others or trying to get them to do wrong.

10.o: The largest desire of eternal blessings the righteous can form, will be granted.
10.m: The righteous will receive all the greatest desires they have for eternal blessings.

10.o: The course of prosperous sinners is like a whirlwind, which soon spends itself, and is gone.
10.m: The path of successful sinners is like a whirlwind that quickly dies out and disappears.

10.o: As vinegar sets the teeth on edge, and as the smoke causes the eyes to smart, so the sluggard vexes his employer.
10.m: Like how vinegar makes your teeth hurt or smoke stings your eyes, a lazy worker bothers their boss.

10.o: What man is he that loves life? Let him fear God, and that will secure to him life enough in this world, and eternal life in the other.
10.m: Who wants to love life? Fear God, and he will give you enough life in this world and eternal life in the next.

10.o: The believer grows stronger in faith, and obeys with increased delight.
10.m: The believer's faith grows stronger, and they obey with more joy.

10.o: The wicked would be glad to have this earth their home for ever, but it cannot be so. They must die and leave all their idols behind.
10.m: The wicked want this earth to be their home forever, but it cannot be. They will die and leave all their idols behind.

10.o: A good man discourses wisely for the benefit of others. But it is the sin, and will be the ruin of a wicked man, that he speaks what is displeasing to God, and provoking to those he converses with. The righteous is kept by the power of God; and nothing shall be able to separate him from the love of God which is in Christ Jesus.
10.m: A good person talks wisely to help others. A bad person, however, sins by saying things that upset God and annoy those around them. The righteous person is protected by God’s power. Nothing can pull them away from God’s love found in Christ Jesus.

11.o: However men may make light of giving short weight or measure, and however common such crimes may be, they are an abomination to the Lord.
11.m: Even if people think it’s not a big deal to cheat by giving less than the full amount of weight or measure, God hates these kinds of dishonest practices.

11.o: Considering how safe, and quiet, and easy the humble are, we see that with the lowly is wisdom.
11.m: We see that wise people are humble because they are safe, peaceful, and comfortable.

11.o: An honest man's principles are fixed, therefore his way is plain.
11.m: An honest person knows what they believe, so their path is clear.

11.o: Riches will stand men in no stead in the day of death.
11.m: Money won't help you on the day you die.

11.o: The ways of wickedness are dangerous. And sin will be its own punishment.
11.m: Wicked ways are dangerous. Sin will bring its own punishment.

11.o: When a godly man dies, all his fears vanish; but when a wicked man dies, his hopes vanish.
11.m: When a good person dies, their fears go away. When a bad person dies, their hopes disappear.

11.o: The righteous are often wonderfully kept from going into dangerous situations, and the ungodly go in their stead.
11.m: Righteous people are often protected from dangerous situations, while those who are not godly sometimes face them instead.

11.o: Hypocrites delude men into error and sin by artful objections against the truths of God's word.
11.m: Hypocrites trick people into making mistakes and sinning by raising clever arguments against what God's word says.

11.o: Nations prosper when wicked men are cast down.
11.m: Countries do well when bad people are removed.

11.o: A man of understanding does not judge of others by their success.
11.m: A wise person doesn’t judge others based on their success.

11.o: A faithful man will not disclose what he is trusted with, unless the honour of God and the real good of society require it.
11.m: A honest person won't share secrets they were told unless it's necessary to protect God's honor or help others.

11.o: We shall often find it to our advantage to advise with others.
11.m: It is often helpful to talk things over with other people.

11.o: The welfare of our families, our own peace, and our ability to pay just debts, must not be brought into danger. But here especially let us consider the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ in becoming Surety even for enemies.
11.m: Our family's well-being, our personal peace, and our ability to pay what we owe should not be put at risk. We should especially think about the kindness of Jesus Christ, who became responsible even for his enemies.

11.o: A pious and discreet woman will keep esteem and respect, as strong men keep possession of wealth.
11.m: A godly and careful woman will earn respect, just as a wise man keeps his wealth safe.

11.o: A cruel, froward, ill-natured man, is vexatious to those that are, and should be to him as his own flesh, and punishes himself.
11.m: A mean and unpleasant person causes trouble for people who should be close to them, like family. They end up hurting themselves too.

11.o: He that makes it his business to do good, shall have a reward, as sure to him as eternal truth can make it.
11.m: The person who focuses on doing good will receive a reward, just as surely as eternal truth promises it.

11.o: True holiness is true happiness. The more violent a man is in sinful pursuits, the more he hastens his own destruction.
11.m: True holiness leads to true happiness. The more someone tries to satisfy their sins, the faster they bring ruin on themselves.

11.o: Nothing is more hateful to God, than hypocrisy and double dealing, which are here signified. God delights in such as aim and act with uprightness.
11.m: God hates hypocrisy and lying the most. He loves people who are honest and do what they say they will do.

11.o: Joining together in sin shall not protect the sinners.
11.m: Being involved in the same wrongdoings will not save sinners.

11.o: Beauty is abused by those who have not discretion or modesty with it. This is true of all bodily endowments.
11.m: People misuse their good looks if they lack common sense or modesty. This applies to all physical advantages.

11.o: The wicked desire mischief to others, but it shall return upon themselves.
11.m: Bad people want to cause trouble for others, but the trouble will come back on them instead.

11.o: A man may grow poor by not paying just debts, not relieving the poor, not allowing needful expenses. Let men be ever so saving of what they have, if God appoints, it comes to nothing.
11.m: A person might become poor by not paying their debts fairly, not helping those in need, or not spending money on necessary things. Even if people try to save everything they have, if God decides otherwise, it will come to nothing.

11.o: Both in temporal and spiritual things, God commonly deals with his people according to the measure by which they deal with their brethren.
11.m: In both everyday matters and spiritual ones, God usually treats his people the way they treat each other.

11.o: We must not hoard up the gifts of God's bounty, merely for our own advantage.
11.m: We should not keep the gifts from God all to ourselves.

11.o: Seeking mischief is here set against seeking good; for those that are not doing good are doing hurt, even to themselves.
11.m: Looking for trouble is compared to looking for good things. People who don't do good things end up hurting themselves.

11.o: The true believer is a branch of the living Vine. When those that take root in the world wither, those who are grafted into Christ shall be fruitful.
11.m: A true believer is like a branch of a living vine. While people who focus on the world fade away, those connected to Christ will produce fruit.

11.o: He that brings trouble upon himself and his family, by carelessness, or by wickedness, shall be unable to keep and enjoy what he gets, as a man is unable to hold the wind, or to satisfy himself with it.
11.m: Anyone who causes problems for themselves or their family because of carelessness or bad behavior will not be able to keep what they have. It’s like trying to catch the wind—impossible to hold onto or enjoy.

11.o: The righteous are as trees of life; and their influence upon earth, like the fruits of that tree, support and nourish the spiritual life in many.
11.m: The righteous are like trees of life. Their good influence helps support and nourish the spiritual lives of many people.

11.o: Even the righteous, when they offend on earth, shall meet with sharp corrections; much more will the wicked meet the due reward of their sins. Let us then seek those blessings which our Surety purchased by his sufferings and death; let us seek to copy his example, and to keep his commandments.
11.m: Even good people sometimes get punished for their mistakes. So imagine how much worse it will be for those who do bad things. We should try to get the blessings Jesus earned for us through his suffering and death. We should follow his example and obey his rules.

12.o: Those who have grace, will delight in the instructions given them. Those that stifle their convictions, are like brutes.
12.m: People with grace enjoy learning from what they are taught. Those who ignore their conscience are like animals.

12.o: The man who covers selfish and vicious designs under a profession of religion or friendship, will be condemned.
12.m: A person who hides evil plans behind a mask of religion or friendship will be punished.

12.o: Though men may advance themselves by sinful arts, they cannot settle and secure themselves. But those who by faith are rooted in Christ, are firmly fixed.
12.m: Even if people use bad methods to get ahead, they cannot stay secure. But those who trust in Christ are firmly established.

12.o: A wife who is pious, prudent, and looks well to the ways of her household, who makes conscience of her duty, and can bear crosses; such a one is an honour and comfort to her husband. She that is the reverse of this, preys upon him, and consumes him.
12.m: A wife who is religious, wise, and takes care of her home; she does her duties carefully and can handle difficulties. Such a wife brings honor and joy to her husband. But if a wife is the opposite—irreligious, foolish, and careless—she harms her husband instead.

12.o: Thoughts are not free; they are under the Divine knowledge, therefore under the Divine command. It is a man's shame to act with deceit, with trick and design.
12.m: Thoughts are not free because God knows them. They are under his control. It is shameful for a person to be dishonest, using tricks and plans.

12.o: Wicked people speak mischief to their neighbours. A man may sometimes do a good work with one good word.
12.m:
Bad people say mean things about their neighbors.
Sometimes a kind word can do a lot of good.

12.o: God's blessing is often continued to the families of godly men, while the wicked are overthrown.
12.m: God often blesses the families of good people, while he brings down the wicked.

12.o: The apostles showed wisdom by glorying in shame for the name of Christ.
12.m: The apostles showed wisdom by being proud of the trouble they faced for following Christ.

12.o: He that lives in a humble state, who has no one to wait upon him, but gets bread by his own labour, is happier than he that glories in high birth or gay attire, and wants necessaries.
12.m: A person who lives simply, without servants, and earns their bread through hard work is happier than someone who boasts about their fancy clothes or important family and lacks basic needs.

12.o: A godly man would not put even an animal to needless pain. But the wicked often speak of others as well used, when they would not endure like treatment for a single day.
12.m: A good person wouldn’t cause unnecessary pain to an animal. But evil people often say others are treated well, even though they wouldn’t want to be treated that way themselves for one day.

12.o: It is men's wisdom to mind their business, and follow an honest calling. But it is folly to neglect business; and the grace of God teaches men to disdain nothing but sin.
12.m: It's wise for people to focus on their work and have an honest job. It's foolish to ignore their duties. The grace of God helps people avoid only what is sinful.

12.o: When the ungodly see others prosper by sin, they wish they could act in the same way. But the root of Divine grace, in the heart of the righteous, produces other desires and purposes.
12.m: When wicked people see others succeed through sin, they want to do the same things. But God’s grace in the hearts of good people creates different wishes and goals.

12.o: Many a man has paid dear in this world for the transgression of his lips.
12.m: Many people have suffered in this world because of things they said.

12.o: When men use their tongues aright, to teach and comfort others, they enjoy acceptance through Christ Jesus; and the testimony of their conscience, that they in some measure answer the end of their being.
12.m: When people use their words to teach and comfort others, they find favor through Christ Jesus. Their conscience also testifies that they are fulfilling the purpose for which they exist.

12.o: A fool, in the sense of Scripture, means a wicked man, one who acts contrary to the wisdom that is from above. His rule is, to do what is right in his own eyes.
12.m: A fool, as the Bible uses the word, means someone who is wicked. This person does things against God’s wisdom. Their rule is to do what seems right to them.

12.o: A foolish man is soon angry, and is hasty in expressing it; he is ever in trouble and running into mischief. It is kindness to ourselves to make light of injuries and affronts, instead of making the worst of them.
12.m: A foolish person gets angry quickly and acts on it right away. They often get themselves into trouble because of their anger. It’s good for us to not take offenses too seriously and to let them go rather than dwelling on them.

12.o: It is good for all to dread and detest the sin of lying, and to be governed by honesty.
12.m: Everyone should fear and hate lying, and always be honest.

12.o: Whisperings and evil surmises, like a sword, separate those that have been dear to each other. The tongue of the wise is health, making all whole.
12.m: Gossip and bad thoughts can split apart people who were close friends. Wise people use their words to heal relationships.

12.o: If truth be spoken, it will hold good; whoever may be disobliged, still it will keep its ground.
12.m: If the truth is told, it will stand strong. It won't matter if some people get upset—it will still stay true.

12.o: Deceit and falsehood bring terrors and perplexities. But those who consult the peace and happiness of others have joy in their own minds.
12.m: Deception and lies cause fear and confusion. But people who care about others' peace and happiness feel joy inside.

12.o: If men are sincerely righteous, the righteous God has engaged that no evil shall happen to them. But they that delight in mischief shall have enough of it.
12.m: If people are truly good, God promises that nothing bad will happen to them. But those who enjoy doing wrong will get what they deserve.

12.o: Make conscience of truth, not only in words, but in actions.
12.m: Be honest in both what you say and what you do.

12.o: Foolish men proclaim to all the folly and emptiness of their minds.
12.m: Foolish people show everyone how empty their minds are.

12.o: Those who will not take pains in an honest calling, living by tricks and dishonesty, are paltry and beggarly.
12.m: People who refuse to work honestly and earn money fairly by cheating instead are cheap and poor.

12.o: Care, fear, and sorrow, upon the spirits, deprive men of vigour in what is to be done, or courage in what is to be borne. A good word from God, applied by faith, makes the heart glad.
12.m: Worry, fear, and sadness can drain our energy and courage. But hearing a good message from God, and believing it, can cheer us up.

12.o: The righteous is abundant; though not in this world's goods, yet in the graces and comforts of the Spirit, which are the true riches. Evil men vainly flatter themselves that their ways are not wrong.
12.m: The righteous person has plenty, even if not in worldly things. They have an abundance of spiritual gifts and comfort, which are true treasures. Bad people fool themselves by thinking their ways are fine.

12.o: The slothful man makes no good use of the advantages Providence puts in his way, and has no comfort in them. The substance of a diligent man, though not great, does good to him and his family. He sees that God gives it to him in answer to prayer.
12.m:
A lazy person doesn’t use the chances given by God to do well. They don’t enjoy those chances either. 

But an hardworking person’s earnings, even if small, help them and their family. They see that God gives these things because they prayed for them.

12.o: The way of religion is a straight, plain way; it is the way of righteousness. There is not only life at the end, but life in the way; all true comfort.
12.m: The path of religion is straightforward and clear. It is the path of doing what is right. There is more than just reward at the end; there is real comfort along the whole journey.

13.o: There is great hope of those that reverence their parents. There is little hope of any who will not hear those that deal faithfully with them.
13.m:
Those who respect their parents have much to be hopeful about.
There is little hope for people who refuse to listen to those who give them honest advice.

13.o: By our words we must be justified or condemned, Mt 12:37.
13.m: Our words will determine whether we are judged fairly or unfairly, Matthew 12:37.

13.o: He that thinks before he speaks, that suppresses evil if he have thought it, keeps his soul from a great deal both of guilt and grief. Many a one is ruined by an ungoverned tongue.
13.m: The person who thinks before speaking and stops himself from saying bad things avoids a lot of trouble and sadness. Many people get into trouble because they don't control what they say.

13.o: The slothful desire the gains the diligent get, but hate the pains the diligent take; therefore they have nothing. This is especially true as to the soul.
13.m:
Lazy people want what hardworking people earn, but they hate the effort hardworking people put in. So lazy people end up with nothing.

This is even more true for spiritual things.

13.o: Where sin reigns, the man is loathsome. If his conscience were awake, he would abhor himself, and repent in dust and ashes.
13.m: When sin controls someone, they become repulsive. If their conscience was active, they would hate themselves and feel sorry enough to repent.

13.o: An honest desire to do right, preserves a man from fatal mistakes, better than a thousand fine-drawn distinctions.
13.m: A sincere wish to do what’s right helps a person avoid big mistakes better than knowing many detailed rules.

13.o: Some who are really poor, trade and spend as if they were rich: this is sin, and will be shame, and it will end accordingly. Some that are really rich, would be thought to be poor: in this there is want of gratitude to God, want of justice and charity to others. There are many hypocrites, empty of grace, who will not be convinced of their poverty. There are many fearing Christians, who are spiritually rich, yet think themselves poor; by their doubts, and complaints, and griefs, they make themselves poor.
13.m:
Some people who are actually poor act and spend money like they are rich. This is wrong and will lead to shame. 

Other people who are truly rich pretend to be poor. This shows a lack of thankfulness to God and a lack of fairness and kindness to others.

There are many hypocrites—people without true faith—who refuse to see their spiritual emptiness. 

Then there are fearful believers who have real spiritual wealth but think they are poor. They feel sad and worried, which makes them believe they don’t have enough.

13.o: Great riches often tempt to violence against those that possess them; but the poor are free from such perils.
13.m: Rich people often face danger because others might try to take their money by force. Poor people do not have this problem.

13.o: The light of the righteous is as that of the sun, which may be eclipsed and clouded, but will continue: the Spirit is their Light, he gives a fulness of joy: that of the wicked is as a lamp of their own kindling, easily put out.
13.m: The light of the righteous is like the sun. It might get blocked by clouds, but it keeps shining. The Holy Spirit is their light, giving them full joy. But the light of the wicked is like a small lamp they made themselves. It can easily go out.

13.o: All contentions, whether between private persons, families, churches, or nations, are begun and carried forward by pride. Disputes would be easily prevented or ended, if it were not for pride.
13.m: All arguments, whether between individuals, families, churches, or countries, start and continue because of pride. Arguments could be easily avoided or stopped if people weren't so proud.

13.o: Wealth gotten by dishonesty or vice, has a secret curse, which will speedily waste it.
13.m: Money gained through dishonesty or bad behavior comes with a hidden curse that will quickly ruin it.

13.o: The delay of what is anxiously hoped for, is very painful to the mind; obtaining it is very pleasant. But spiritual blessings are chiefly intended.
13.m: Waiting for something you hope for can be very hard on your mind. Getting it makes you feel good. But spiritual blessings are the most important ones.

13.o: He that stands in awe of God, and reverences his word, shall escape destruction, and be rewarded for his godly fear.
13.m: If you respect God and honor his word, you will avoid harm and be rewarded for your reverence.

13.o: The rule by which the wise regulate their conduct, is a fountain yielding life and happiness.
13.m: The wise people follow a rule that brings them life and happiness.

13.o: The way of sinners is hard upon others, and hard to the sinner himself. The service of sin is slavery; the road to hell is strewed with the thorns and thistles that followed the curse.
13.m: The path of sinners is tough on others and tough for themselves too. Serving sin is like being a slave. The road to hell is full of the thorns and thistles that came after the curse.

13.o: It is folly to talk of things of which we know nothing, and to undertake what we are no way fit for.
13.m: It's silly to talk about things we don't know anything about, and to try to do things we're not suited for.

13.o: Those that are wicked, and false to Christ and to the souls of men, do mischief, and fall into mischief; but those that are faithful, find sound words healing to others and to themselves.
13.m: People who are bad and betray Christ and harm others end up causing trouble for themselves. But those who are loyal find helpful, healing words that benefit both others and themselves.

13.o: He that scorns to be taught, will certainly be brought down.
13.m: He who refuses to learn will surely be brought down.

13.o: There are in man strong desires after happiness; but never let those expect any thing truly sweet to their souls, who will not be persuaded to leave their sins.
13.m: People have strong wishes for happiness. But those who refuse to stop sinning should not expect true joy in their hearts.

13.o: Multitudes are brought to ruin by bad company. And all that make themselves wicked will be destroyed.
13.m: Many people get ruined because of bad friends. Anyone who becomes evil will be destroyed.

13.o: When God pursues sinners he is sure to overtake them; and he will reward the righteous.
13.m: When God chases sinners, he always catches up to them. He also rewards those who do right.

13.o: The servant of God who is not anxious about riches, takes the best method of providing for his children.
13.m: A person who trusts in God and isn't worried about money is doing the best thing for their children.

13.o: The poor, yet industrious, thrive, though in a homely manner, while those who have great riches are often brought to poverty for want of judgment.
13.m: The hardworking poor do well, even if modestly, while those with lots of money often become poor because they lack good judgment.

13.o: He acts as if he hated his child, who, by false indulgence, permits sinful habits to gather strength, which will bring sorrow here, and misery hereafter.
13.m: He treats his child badly by letting them get used to bad behaviors, which will cause trouble now and problems later.

13.o: It is the misery of the wicked, that even their sensual appetites are always craving. The righteous feeds on the word and ordinances, to the satisfying of his soul with the promises of the gospel, and the Lord Jesus Christ, who is the Bread of life.
13.m: The problem with evil people is that their desires for pleasure are never satisfied. Righteous people find fulfillment by feeding on God’s word and the teachings of the church. They satisfy their souls with the promises from the gospel and by focusing on Jesus Christ, who is called the Bread of Life.

14.o: A woman who has no fear of God, who is wilful and wasteful, and indulges her ease, will as certainly ruin her family, as if she plucked her house down.
14.m: A woman who doesn't fear God, who is stubborn and wasteful, and enjoys living comfortably, will surely destroy her family just as much as if she were tearing her house apart.

14.o: Here are grace and sin in their true colours. Those that despise God's precepts and promises, despise God and all his power and mercy.
14.m: Here we see grace and sin clearly shown. People who ignore God’s commands and promises are really ignoring God himself and all his power and mercy.

14.o: Pride grows from that root of bitterness which is in the heart. The root must be plucked up, or we cannot conquer this branch. The prudent words of wise men get them out of difficulties.
14.m: Pride comes from the bitter feelings inside us. We need to get rid of those feelings if we want to control our pride. Wise people use their smart words to solve problems.

14.o: There can be no advantage without something which, though of little moment, will affright the indolent.
14.m: There can be no benefit without something small but scary enough to wake up lazy people.

14.o: A conscientious witness will not dare to represent anything otherwise than according to his knowledge.
14.m: A honest witness will not lie about what they know.

14.o: A scorner treats Divine things with contempt. He that feels his ignorance and unworthiness will search the Scriptures in a humble spirit.
14.m:
A person who mocks God’s teachings shows disrespect.
Someone who knows their own limits will study the scriptures with humility.

14.o: We discover a wicked man if there is no savour of piety in his discourse.
14.m: We know someone is bad if their talk doesn't show any kindness or goodness.

14.o: We are travellers, whose concern is, not to spy out wonders, but to get to their journey's end; to understand the rules we are to walk by, also the ends we are to walk toward. The bad man cheats himself, and goes on in his mistake.
14.m: We are travelers who care more about reaching our destination than finding wonders along the way. We need to know the rules to follow and the goals to aim for. A bad person tricks themselves and keeps going in the wrong direction.

14.o: Foolish and profane men consider sin a mere trifle, to be made light of rather than mourned over. Fools mock at the sin-offering; but those that make light of sin, make light of Christ.
14.m: Foolish and rude people think sin is no big deal and laugh about it instead of feeling sorry for it. Fools joke about things that need sacrifices to fix them. But if you don’t take sin seriously, you’re not taking Christ seriously either.

14.o: We do not know what stings of conscience, or consuming passions, torment the prosperous sinner. Nor does the world know the peace of mind a serious Christian enjoys, even in poverty and sickness.
14.m: We don't know the guilt and pain that trouble successful sinners. And the world doesn't know the peace that a serious believer has, even when poor and sick.

14.o: Sin ruins many great families; whilst righteousness often raises and strengthens even mean families.
14.m: Sin destroys many great families, while righteousness often builds up and strengthens even humble families.

14.o: The ways of carelessness, of worldliness, and of sensuality, seem right to those that walk in them; but self-deceivers prove self-destroyers. See the vanity of carnal mirth.
14.m: Carelessness, being too focused on worldly things, and giving in to desires all seem right to people while they do them. But these self-deceptions lead to their own destruction. Notice how empty earthly pleasures really are.

14.o: Of all sinners backsliders will have the most terror when they reflect on their own ways.
14.m: Backsliders will be the most afraid when they think about their past actions.

14.o: Eager readiness to believe what others say, has ever proved mischievous. The whole world was thus ruined at first. The man who is spiritually wise, depends on the Saviour alone for acceptance. He is watchful against the enemies of his salvation, by taking heed to God's word.
14.m: Being too quick to believe what others say often causes trouble. This happened when the world was first created. A person who is spiritually smart relies only on the Savior for approval. They stay alert to avoid anything that could harm their salvation by paying attention to God’s word.

14.o: Holy fear guards against every thing unholy.
14.m: Holy fear protects against everything that is unholy.

14.o: An angry man is to be pitied as well as blamed; but the revengeful is more hateful.
14.m: An angry person should be pitied as well as criticized, but a vengeful person is even more hated.

14.o: Sin is the shame of sinners; but wisdom is the honour of the wise.
14.m: Sin shames sinners, but wisdom honors the wise.
