14.o: Even bad men acknowledge the excellency of God's people.
14.m: Even bad people admit that God's people are excellent.

14.o: Friendship in the world is governed by self-interest. It is good to have God our Friend; he will not desert us.
14.m:
Having God as a friend is good because He will not abandon us.
Friends in the world often stick around only if it benefits them. But God won't leave us.

14.o: To despise a man for his employment or appearance is a sin.
14.m: It’s wrong to look down on someone because of their job or how they look.

14.o: How wisely those consult their own interest, who not only do good, but devise it!
14.m: People help themselves the most when they plan to do good things and then follow through with them.

14.o: Labour of the head, or of the hand, will turn to some good account. But if men's religion runs all out in talk and noise, they will come to nothing.
14.m: Hard work with your mind or hands will lead to something good. But if someone’s religion is just empty talk and noise, it won’t amount to anything.

14.o: The riches of men of wisdom and piety enlarge their usefulness.
14.m: The wealth of wise and pious people makes them more helpful to others.

14.o: An upright man will venture the displeasure of the greatest, to bring truth to light.
14.m: A honest person will face the anger of those in power to reveal the truth.

14.o: Those who fear the Lord so as to obey and serve him, have a strong ground of confidence, and will be preserved. Let us seek to this Fountain of life, that we may escape the snares of death.
14.m: People who respect God by obeying and serving him can feel confident and will be protected. We should go to this source of life so we can avoid the dangers of death.

14.o: Let all that wish well to the kingdom of Christ, do what they can, that many may be added to his church.
14.m: Everyone who wants what’s best for Christ’s kingdom should do what they can to bring more people into his church.

14.o: A mild, patient man is one that learns of Christ, who is Wisdom itself. Unbridled passion is folly made known.
14.m: A gentle and patient person learns from Christ, who is full of wisdom. Losing control of your emotions shows you lack wisdom.

14.o: An upright, contented, and benevolent mind, tends to health.
14.m: A honest, satisfied, and kind attitude helps keep you healthy.

14.o: To oppress the poor is to reproach our Creator.
14.m: Oppressing the poor is insulting our Creator.

14.o: The wicked man has his soul forced from him; he dies in his sins, under the guilt and power of them. But godly men, though they have pain and some dread of death, have the blessed hope, which God, who cannot lie, has given them.
14.m:
The wicked person dies while still guilty of their sins. 
But godly people die with hope, because God, who always tells the truth, promises them something good.

14.o: Wisdom possesses the heart, and thus regulates the affections and tempers.
14.m: Wisdom controls the heart, so it manages feelings and attitudes.

14.o: Piety and holiness always promote industry, sobriety, and honesty.
14.m: Being religious and living a good life encourages people to work hard, be sober, and act honestly.

14.o: The great King who reigns over heaven and earth, will reward faithful servants who honour his gospel by the proper discharge of the duties of their stations: he despises not the services of the lowest.
14.m: The king who rules heaven and earth rewards faithful servants who honor his teachings by doing their jobs well. He doesn't ignore the work of even the most humble people.

15.o: A right cause will be better pleaded with meekness than with passion. Nothing stirs up anger like grievous words.
15.m:
Being meek is better than being angry when you have a good reason to speak up.
Grievous words often make people angry.

15.o: He that has knowledge, is to use it aright, for the good of others.
15.m: A person who has knowledge should use it properly to help others.

15.o: Secret sins, services, and sorrows, are under God's eye. This speaks comfort to saints, and terror to sinners.
15.m: Hidden sins, good deeds, and troubles are all seen by God. This gives comfort to believers and fear to those who sin.

15.o: A good tongue is healing to wounded consciences, by comforting them; to sin-sick souls, by convincing them; and it reconciles parties at variance.
15.m: A kind word heals hurt feelings by comforting people. It helps those who feel guilty by showing them the truth. And it brings together people who are arguing.

15.o: If instruction is despised, reprove men rather than suffer them to go on undisturbed in the way to ruin.
15.m: If people don't listen to advice, correct them instead of letting them continue down a harmful path.

15.o: The wealth of worldly men increases their fears and suspicions, adds strength to their passions, and renders the fear of death more distressing.
15.m: Worldly wealth makes people more afraid and suspicious. It also strengthens their desires and makes them more upset about the thought of dying.

15.o: We use knowledge aright when we disperse it; but the heart of the foolish has nothing to disperse that is good.
15.m: We use knowledge well when we share it. But the foolish person has nothing good to share.

15.o: The wicked put other things in the stead of Christ's atonement, or in the place of holy obedience. Praying graces are his gift, and the work of his Spirit, with which he is well pleased.
15.m: The wicked replace Christ’s atonement with other things, or substitute it for their own sinful actions. Prayer is a gift from God and comes from the work of his Spirit, which pleases him.

15.o: He that hates reproof shall perish in his sins, since he would not be parted from them.
15.m: He who dislikes being corrected will suffer because of his sins since he refused to let go of them.

15.o: There is nothing that can be hid from the eyes of God, not even man's thoughts.
15.m: God can see everything, including what people think.

15.o: A scorner cannot bear to reflect seriously within his own heart.
15.m: A mocker doesn’t like to think seriously about things.

15.o: A gloomy, impatient, unthankful spirit, springing from pride and undue attachment to worldly objects, renders a man uneasy to himself and others.
15.m: An unhappy, impatient, ungrateful attitude, which comes from being too proud and attached to material things, makes someone uncomfortable for themselves and those around them.

15.o: A wise man seeks to gain more wisdom, growing in grace and in the knowledge of Christ. But a carnal mind rests contented, flattering itself.
15.m: A wise person keeps seeking more wisdom, growing in their understanding of God and Jesus. But someone who is focused on worldly things is satisfied with what they know and flatters themselves.

15.o: Some are much in affliction, and of a sorrowful spirit. Such are to be pitied, prayed for, and comforted. And others serve God with gladness of heart, and it prompts their obedience, yet they should rejoice with trembling.
15.m:
Some people face many troubles and feel very sad. We should pity them, pray for them, and try to comfort them.

Others serve God happily, which makes them obey him more. But even if they feel joyful, they should still have some fear and respect.

15.o: Believers often have enough when worldly eyes see little; the Lord is with them, without the cares, troubles, and temptations which are with the wealth of the wicked.
15.m: Believers often have plenty even when others think they don't have much. The Lord is with them, keeping them free from the worries, troubles, and temptations that come with wealth.

15.o: He that is slow to anger, not only prevents strife, but appeases it, if kindled.
15.m: He who is patient and doesn't get angry quickly not only stops arguments from happening but also calms them down if they start.

15.o: Those who have no heart to their work, pretend that they cannot do their work without hardship and danger. And thus many live always in doubt about their state, because always in neglect of some duty.
15.m: People who don't want to do their work say it’s too hard or dangerous. Many people are always unsure about their situation because they often skip duties they should be doing.

15.o: Those who treat an aged mother or a father with contempt or neglect, show their own folly.
15.m: People who disrespect or ignore their elderly parents reveal their own foolishness.

15.o: Such as are truly wise, study that their thoughts, words, and actions should be regular, sincere, and holy.
15.m: People who are truly wise try to make sure their thoughts, words, and actions are proper, honest, and good.

15.o: If men will not take time and pains to deliberate, they are not likely to bring any thing to pass.
15.m: If people don't take the time to think things through carefully, they probably won't succeed.

15.o: Wisdom is needed to suit our discourse to the occasions.
15.m: We need wisdom to speak appropriately for different situations.

15.o: A good man sets his affections on things above; his way leads directly thither.
15.m: A good person focuses on things above. Their path goes straight there.

15.o: Pride is the ruin of multitudes. But those who are in affliction God will support.
15.m: Pride destroys many people, but God helps those who are suffering.

15.o: The thoughts of wicked men offend Him who knows the heart.
15.m: Wicked people's thoughts bother God, who understands what's in their hearts.

15.o: The covetous man lets none of his family have rest or enjoyment. And greediness of gain often tempts to projects that bring ruin.
15.m: The greedy person doesn't let anyone in their family relax or enjoy life. Being too focused on making money often leads to plans that cause ruin.

15.o: A good man is proved to be a wise man by this; he governs his tongue well.
15.m: A good person shows they are wise by controlling what they say.

15.o: God sets himself at a distance from those who set him at defiance.
15.m: God distances himself from those who defy him.

15.o: How delightful to the humbled soul to hear the good report of salvation by the Lord Jesus Christ!
15.m: It is so pleasing for someone who feels humble to hear about the way to be saved through Lord Jesus Christ.

15.o: Faithful, friendly reproofs help spiritual life, and lead to eternal life.
15.m: Friendly and honest corrections help our spiritual growth and lead to eternal life.

15.o: Sinners undervalue their own souls; therefore they prefer the body before the soul, and wrong the soul to please the body.
15.m: People who sin don't think their souls are important. So they care more about their bodies than their souls. They hurt their souls to satisfy their bodies.

15.o: The fear of the Lord will dispose us to search the Scriptures with reverence; and it will cause us to follow the leadings of the Holy Spirit. While we humbly place all our dependence on the grace of God, we are exalted in the righteousness of Christ.
15.m: The fear of the Lord makes us look at the Scriptures with respect. It helps us follow the guidance of the Holy Spirit. As we depend on God's grace, we are made righteous through Christ.

16.o: The renewing grace of God alone prepares the heart for every good work. This teaches us that we are not sufficient of ourselves to think or speak any thing wise and good.
16.m: God's renewing grace alone prepares our hearts for doing good things. This shows us that we cannot think or say anything wise on our own.

16.o: Ignorance, pride, and self-flattery render us partial judges respecting our own conduct.
16.m: Not knowing everything, being proud, and thinking we're better than we are makes us unfair judges of our own actions.

16.o: Roll the burden of thy care upon God, and leave it with him, by faith and dependence on him.
16.m: Put your worries on God, and depend on him through faith.

16.o: God makes use of the wicked to execute righteous vengeance on each other; and he will be glorified by their destruction at last.
16.m: God uses evil people to punish one another. In the end, their destruction will show how great he is.

16.o: Though sinners strengthen themselves and one another, they shall not escape God's judgments.
16.m: Even though sinners try to support each other, they cannot avoid God’s punishment.

16.o: By the mercy and truth of God in Christ Jesus, the sins of believers are taken away, and the power of sin is broken.
16.m: Through God's mercy and truth in Christ Jesus, the sins of believers are forgiven, and the power of sin is broken.

16.o: He that has all hearts in his hand, can make a man's enemies to be at peace with him.
16.m: The one who controls everyone's heart can make an enemy become friendly.

16.o: A small estate, honestly come by, will turn to better account than a great estate ill-gotten.
16.m: A small amount of money gotten honestly is worth more than a large amount gained dishonestly.

16.o: If men make God's glory their end, and his will their rule, he will direct their steps by his Spirit and grace. 10. Let kings and judges of the earth be just, and rule in the fear of God.
16.m:
If people make honoring God their goal and follow his wishes, he will guide them through his Spirit and grace. 

Kings and judges should be fair and rule while fearing God.

16.o: To observe justice in dealings between man and man is God's appointment.
16.m: God wants us to be fair when we deal with each other.

16.o: The ruler that uses his power aright, will find that to be his best security.
16.m: A leader who uses their power correctly will find it gives them the safest protection.

16.o: Put those in power who know how to speak to the purpose.
16.m: Choose leaders who know how to speak clearly and directly.

16.o: Those are fools, who, to obtain the favour of an earthly prince, throw themselves out of God's favour.
16.m: People are foolish if they try to please an earthly ruler by losing God’s favor.

16.o: There is joy and satisfaction of spirit, only in getting wisdom.
16.m: There is joy and satisfaction in gaining wisdom.

16.o: A sincerely religious man keeps at a distance from every appearance of evil. Happy is the man that walks in Christ, and is led by the Spirit of Christ.
16.m: A truly religious person stays away from anything that seems wrong. It’s great for someone who follows Christ and lets the Spirit of Christ guide them.

16.o: When men defy God's judgments, and think themselves far from them, it is a sign they are at the door. Let us not fear the pride of others, but fear pride in ourselves.
16.m: When people ignore God's punishments and think they won't face them, it shows those troubles are near. We should not be afraid of other people's pride, but we should be afraid of our own pride.

16.o: Humility, though it exposes to contempt in the world, is much better than high-spiritedness, which makes God an enemy. He that understands God's word shall find good.
16.m: Humility might lead others to look down on you, but it is better than being proud, which can make God angry. Someone who understands God’s word will find goodness.

16.o: The man whose wisdom dwells in his heart, will be found more truly prudent than many who possess shining talents.
16.m: A person who has wisdom inside them will be more truly smart than many people with bright talents.

16.o: As waters to a thirsty land, so is a wise man to his friends and neighbours.
16.m: A wise person is like water to dry land for their friends and neighbors.

16.o: The wise man's self-knowledge, always suggests something proper to be spoken to others.
16.m: The wise person's understanding of themselves helps them know what to say to others.

16.o: The word of God cures the diseases that weaken our souls.
16.m: God's word heals the problems that weaken our souls.

16.o: This is caution to all, to take heed of deceiving themselves as to their souls.
16.m: This is a warning for everyone to be careful not to fool themselves about their souls.

16.o: We must labour for the meat which endureth to everlasting life, or we must perish.
16.m: We must work for the food that lasts forever, or we will die.

16.o: Ungodly men bestow more pains to do mischief than would be needful to do good. The whisperer separates friends: what a hateful, but how common a character!
16.m: Bad people put more effort into causing harm than they would if they were helping others. Gossips divide friends—how terrible, yet how common!

16.o: Some do all the mischief they can by force and violence, and are blind to the result.
16.m: Some people cause a lot of harm through force and violence, and they don't see the consequences.

16.o: Old people especially should be found in the way of religion and godliness.
16.m: Elderly people should be actively involved in religious activities and living a godly life.

16.o: To overcome our own passions, requires more steady management, than obtaining victory over an enemy.
16.m: Controlling our own emotions takes more effort than defeating an enemy.

16.o: All the disposal of Providence concerning our affairs, we must look upon to be the determining what we referred to God; and we must be reconciled to them accordingly. Blessed are those that give themselves up to the will of God; for he knows what is good for them.
16.m: We should see everything that happens to us as God’s decision on what we asked him about. We need to accept it calmly. Those who let God guide their lives are happy because he always knows what is best for them.

17.o: These words recommend family love and peace, as needful for the comfort of human life.
17.m: These words tell us that loving and getting along with our family members is important for having a happy life.

17.o: The wise servant is more deserving, and more likely to appear one of the family, than a profligate son.
17.m: A wise servant is more deserving and seems more like part of the family than a wasteful son.

17.o: God tries the heart by affliction. He thus has often shown the sin remaining in the heart of the believer.
17.m: God tests people's hearts through hard times. He has done this many times to show the sin still present in the hearts of believers.

17.o: Flatterers, especially false teachers, are welcome to those that live in sin.
17.m: People who flatter others, especially fake teachers, are often welcomed by those who live in sin.

17.o: Those that laugh at poverty, treat God's providence and precepts with contempt.
17.m: People who laugh at poverty are showing disrespect to God's plans and rules.

17.o: It is an honour to children to have wise and godly parents continued to them, even after they are grown up and settled in the world.
17.m: It is a honor for children to have wise and godly parents even after they grow up and settle in life.

17.o: A fool, in Solomon's Proverbs, signifies a wicked man, whom excellent speech does not become, because his conversation contradicts it.
17.m: In Solomon's Proverbs, a fool means a wicked person. Excellent speech doesn't suit them because their actions don't match it.

17.o: Those who set their hearts upon money, will do any thing for it. What influence should the gifts of God have on our hearts!
17.m: People who really love money will do anything to get it. How should God’s gifts affect our hearts?

17.o: The way to preserve peace is to make the best of every thing; not to notice what has been said or done against ourselves.
17.m: To keep peace, we should focus on the good parts of every situation. We shouldn't pay attention to things people have said or done that might be against us.

17.o: A gentle reproof will enter, not only into the head, but into the heart of a wise man.
17.m: A gentle correction will go beyond just being heard; it will be understood by a wise person.

17.o: Satan, and the messengers of Satan, shall be let loose upon an evil man.
17.m: Satan and the messengers of Satan will be set free to trouble an evil person.

17.o: Let us watch over our own passions, and avoid the company of furious men.
17.m: We should keep an eye on our emotions and stay away from people who get angry easily.

17.o: To render evil for good is devilish. He that does so, brings a curse upon his family.
17.m: Doing bad things in response to good ones is devilish. People who do this bring a curse on their family.

17.o: What danger there is in the beginning of strife! Resist its earliest display; and leave it off, if it were possible, before you begin.
17.m: Be careful at the start of an argument. Stop it early if you can.

17.o: It is an offence to God to acquit the guilty, or to condemn those who are not guilty.
17.m: It offends God when we let guilty people go free or punish innocent people.

17.o: Man's neglect of God's favour and his own interest is very absurd.
17.m: It's silly for people to ignore God's kindness and their own best interests.

17.o: No change of outward circumstances should abate our affection for our friends or relatives. But no friend, except Christ, deserves unlimited confidence. In Him this text did receive, and still receives its most glorious fulfilment.
17.m: Our feelings for friends and family shouldn't change just because their situation changes. But only Christ deserves complete trust. This verse shows how true that is with Jesus.

17.o: Let not any wrong their families. Yet Christ's becoming Surety for men, was a glorious display of Divine wisdom; for he was able to discharge the bond.
17.m: Don't mistreat your family. But when Christ became our guarantor, it showed great wisdom because he had the power to fulfill his promise.

17.o: If we would keep a clear conscience and a quiet mind, we must shun all excitements to anger. And a man who affects a style of living above his means, goes the way to ruin.
17.m: To have a clear conscience and a peaceful mind, we should avoid things that make us angry. Someone who tries to live beyond their means heads down a path to ruin.

17.o: There is nothing got by ill designs. And many have paid dear for an unbridled tongue.
17.m: There is no gain from bad plans. Many people have suffered because they couldn't control their words.

17.o: This speaks very plainly what many wise and good men feel very strongly, how grievous it is to have a foolish, wicked child.
17.m: This clearly shows how many wise and good people feel very strongly about having a foolish and wicked child.

17.o: It is great mercy that God gives us leave to be cheerful, and cause to be cheerful, if by his grace he gives us hearts to be cheerful.
17.m: It is a great kindness that God lets us be happy and gives us reasons to be joyful, especially if he helps our hearts to feel cheerful.

17.o: The wicked are ready to part with their money, though loved, that they may not suffer for their crimes.
17.m: The wicked are quick to give up their money, even if they love it, so they don’t get punished for their wrongdoings.

17.o: The prudent man keeps the word of God continually in view. But the foolish man cannot fix his thoughts, nor pursue any purpose with steadiness.
17.m: A wise person always thinks about God’s word. A foolish person has trouble focusing and cannot stay committed to anything.

17.o: Wicked children despise the authority of their father, and the tenderness of their mother.
17.m: Bad children don't respect their father's authority or their mother's kindness.

17.o: It is very wrong to find fault for doing what is duty.
17.m: It is wrong to complain about doing what you should do.

17.o: A man may show himself to be a wise man, by the good temper of his mind, and by the good government of his tongue. He is careful when he does speak, to speak to the purpose. God knows his heart, and the folly that is bound there; therefore he cannot be deceived in his judgment as men may be.
17.m: A person shows wisdom through their calm mindset and well-controlled speech. They think carefully before speaking and say things that matter. God understands what’s in someone’s heart, even the silly thoughts. So God won’t be fooled by them, unlike people might be.

18.o: If we would get knowledge and grace, we must try all methods of improving ourselves.
18.m: To gain wisdom and God's favor, we should use every method to improve ourselves.

18.o: Those make nothing to purpose, of learning or religion, whose only design is to have something to make a show with.
18.m: People who study a lot just to look good don’t really learn anything meaningful or become religious for the right reasons.

18.o: As soon as sin entered, shame followed.
18.m: As soon as sin came, shame followed.

18.o: The well-spring of wisdom in the heart of a believer, continually supplies words of wisdom.
18.m: The heart of a believer constantly provides wise words.

18.o: The merits of a cause must be looked to, not the person.
18.m: We should look at whether someone is right, not who they are.

18.o: What mischief bad men do to themselves by their ungoverned tongues!
18.m: How much trouble bad people cause themselves by not controlling what they say!

18.o: How base are those that sow contention! and what fatal effects may be expected from small beginnings of jealousy!
18.m: Those who cause arguments are very mean. We should expect bad results from even small starts of jealousy.

18.o: Omissions of duty, and in duty, are fatal to the soul, as well as commissions of sin.
18.m: Failing to do what we should do is just as harmful to our souls as doing things we shouldn't.

18.o: The Divine power, made known in and through our Lord Jesus Christ, forms a strong tower for the believer, who relies on the Lord. How deceitful the defence of the rich man, who has his portion and treasure in this world! It is a strong city and a high wall only in his own conceit; for it will fail when most in need. They will be exposed to the just wrath of that Judge whom they despised as a Saviour.
18.m: The power of God, shown through Jesus Christ, is like a safe tower for those who trust in the Lord. But the protection of a rich person, who keeps their wealth here on earth, isn't real. It feels strong and secure to them, but it won't help when they really need it. The rich will face the anger of the Judge they ignored as their Savior.

18.o: After the heart has been lifted up with pride, a fall comes. But honour shall be the reward of humility.
18.m: After someone becomes proud, they often face failure. However, honor goes to those who stay humble.

18.o: Eagerness, with self-conceit, will expose to shame.
18.m: Being too eager and thinking too highly of yourself can lead to embarrassment.

18.o: Firmness of mind supports under many pains and trials. But when the conscience is tortured with remorse, no human fortitude can bear the misery; what then will hell be?
18.m: Staying strong helps us deal with many kinds of pain and trouble. But when our conscience troubles us with guilt, nothing we do can ease the suffering. So, what will it be like in hell?

18.o: We must get knowledge, not only into our heads, but into our hearts.
18.m: We need to understand things not just in our minds, but also in our hearts.

18.o: Blessed be the Lord, who makes us welcome to come to his throne, without money and without price. May his gifts make room for him in our souls.
18.m: Praise the Lord for letting us approach his throne freely, without cost. May his gifts open up space in our hearts for him.

18.o: It is well to listen to our enemies, that we may form a better judgment of ourselves.
18.m: It's good to listen to our enemies so we can understand ourselves better.

18.o: It was customary sometimes to refer matters to God, by casting lots, with solemn prayer. The profaning the lot, by using it in matters of diversion, or coveting what belongs to others, forms an objection to this now.
18.m: Sometimes people would ask God for guidance by casting lots while praying seriously. Using lots as a game or to get what belongs to someone else is wrong.

18.o: Great care must be taken to prevent quarrels among relations and those under obligations to each other. Wisdom and grace make it easy to forgive; but corruption makes it difficult.
18.m: We need to be very careful to avoid arguments between family members and people who owe loyalty to one another. Wisdom and kindness help us forgive easily, but our natural faults often make it hard.

18.o: The belly is here put for the heart, as elsewhere; and what that is filled with, our satisfaction will be accordingly, and our inward peace.
18.m: The belly represents the heart here, just as in other places. What fills our heart determines our satisfaction and inner peace.

18.o: Many a one has caused his own death, or the death of others, by a false or injurious tongue.
18.m: Many people have caused their own death or the death of others because of lying or saying mean things.

18.o: A good wife is a great blessing to a man, and it is a token of Divine favour.
18.m: A good wife is a big blessing for a man, and it shows God's favor.

18.o: Poverty tells men they must not order or demand. And at the throne of God's grace we are all poor, and must use entreaties.
18.m: Being poor means you can't command things. At God's throne of grace, we are all poor and must ask humbly.

18.o: Christ Jesus never will forsake those who trust in and love him. May we be such friends to others, for our Master's sake. Having loved his own, which were in the world, he loved them unto the end; and we are his friends if we do whatever he commands us, Joh 15:14.
18.m: Christ Jesus will never abandon those who trust in and love him. Let us be good friends to others because of our Master. He loved his followers in the world until the very end. We are his friends if we follow all his commands, John 15:14.

19.o: A poor man who fears God, is more honourable and happy, than a man without wisdom and grace, however rich or advanced in rank.
19.m: A poor person who fears God is more honorable and happy than a rich person without wisdom and grace.

19.o: What good can the soul do, if without knowledge? And he sins who will not take time to ponder the path of his feet.
19.m: What good can the soul gain without knowledge? It is wrong not to think about where you are going.

19.o: Men run into troubles by their own folly, and then fret at the appointments of God.
19.m: People get into trouble because of their own foolishness, and then they complain about God's plans.

19.o: Here we may see how strong is men's love of money.
19.m: We can see here how much people love money.

19.o: Those that tell lies in discourse, are in a fair way to be guilty of bearing false-witness.
19.m: People who lie in conversations are likely to end up telling false stories.

19.o: We are without excuse if we do not love God with all our hearts. His gifts to us are past number, and all the gifts of men to us are fruits of his bounty.
19.m: We have no excuse for not loving God with all our heart. He has given us so many gifts that we cannot count them. All the good things people give us come from his kindness.

19.o: Christ was left by all his disciples; but the Father was with him. It encourages our faith that he had so large an experience of the sorrows of poverty.
19.m: All of Christ’s followers left him, but the Father stayed with him. This helps us believe that Jesus knew what it was like to be very poor and suffer a lot.

19.o: Those only love their souls aright that get true wisdom.
19.m: People love themselves properly only if they gain true wisdom.

19.o: Lying is a damning, destroying sin.
19.m: Lying is a very harmful sin.

19.o: A man that has not wisdom and grace, has no right or title to true joy. It is very unseemly for one who is a servant to sin, to oppress God's free-men.
19.m: A person without wisdom and grace does not have the right to real joy. It looks wrong for someone who serves sin to mistreat those set free by God.

19.o: He attains the most true glory who endeavours most steadily to overcome evil with good.
19.m: The person who truly achieves glory works hardest to overcome evil by doing good.

19.o: Christ is a King, whose wrath against his enemies will be as the roaring of a lion, and his favour to his people as the refreshing dew.
19.m: Christ is a king who shows anger toward his enemies like a roaring lion. But he treats his people with kindness, like gentle dew on the grass.

19.o: It shows the vanity of the world, that we are liable to the greatest griefs where we promise ourselves the greatest comfort.
19.m: It shows how empty the world is that we can face our biggest sorrows where we expect the most happiness.

19.o: A discreet and virtuous wife is more valuable than house and riches.
19.m: A wise and good wife is more valuable than a house or money.

19.o: A sluggish, slothful disposition makes men poor; it brings them to want. And this applies both to the present life and that which is to come.
19.m: A lazy attitude makes people poor. It leads them to lack things. This is true for both this life and the next life.

19.o: If we keep God's word, God's word will keep us from every thing really hurtful. We abuse the doctrine of free grace, if we think that it does away the necessity and advantage of obedience. Those that live at random must die. This truth is clearly taught in words enough to alarm the stoutest sinner.
19.m: If we follow God's commands, his word will protect us from real harm. We misuse the idea of God's grace if we think it means we don't need to obey him. People who live without purpose will face consequences. The Bible teaches this clearly, warning even the strongest sinners.

19.o: God has chosen the poor of this world, to be rich in faith, and heirs of his kingdom.
19.m: God chose the poor people in this world to be rich in faith and heirs of his kingdom.

19.o: When parents keep under foolish tenderness, they do their best to render children a comfort to them, and happy in themselves.
19.m: When parents are overly protective of their children, they try to make sure those children are happy and bring them joy.

19.o: The spared and spoiled child is likely to become a man of great wrath.
19.m: A child who is spared punishment and allowed to do whatever they want is likely to grow up to be very angry.

19.o: Those that would be wise in their latter end, must be taught and ruled when young.
19.m: Those who want to be wise when they are older need to be taught and guided when they are young.

19.o: What should we desire, but that all our purposes may agree with God's holy will?
19.m: What do we want more than for all our plans to match God's perfect will?

19.o: It is far better to have a heart to do good, and want ability for it, than to have ability for it, and want a heart to it.
19.m: It is much better to want the ability to do good things but have the desire to do them than to have the ability to do good things but lack the desire to do them.

19.o: Those that live in the fear of God, shall get safety, satisfaction, and true and complete happiness.
19.m: People who fear God will find safety, satisfaction, and true happiness.

19.o: Indolence, when indulged, so grows upon people, that they have no heart to do the most needful things for themselves.
19.m: When people get lazy and let it continue, they lose motivation to do even the most important things for themselves.

19.o: A gentle rebuke goes farthest with a man of understanding.
19.m: A kind correction works best with someone who understands.

19.o: The young man who wastes his father's substance, or makes his aged mother destitute, is hateful, and will come to disgrace.
19.m: A young person who spends their parents' money badly or leaves their elderly mom without support is hated and will end up disgraced.

19.o: It is the wisdom of young men to dread hearing such talk as puts loose and evil principles into the mind.
19.m: Young men should be wise and avoid listening to talks that have bad ideas.

19.o: Those are the worst of sinners, who are glad of an opportunity to sin.
19.m: The worst sinners are those who are happy to have chances to sin.

19.o: The unbelief of man shall not make God's threatenings of no effect. Christ himself, when bearing sins not his own, was not spared. Justice and judgment took hold of our blessed Surety; and will God spare obstinate sinners?
19.m: God's threats won't be canceled because people don't believe them. Even Jesus, who didn't deserve it, wasn't spared when he carried our sins. Justice came for our Savior. So will God spare those who keep sinning stubbornly?

20.o: It seems hard to believe that men of the greatest abilities, as well as the ignorant, should render themselves fools and madmen, merely for the taste or excitement produced by strong liquors.
20.m: It is hard to believe that both smart people and those who know little would act foolishly just for the pleasure and excitement from drinking strong alcohol.

20.o: How formidable kings are to those who provoke them! how much more foolish then is it to provoke the King of kings!
20.m: Kings are very scary to people who anger them. It's even sillier to anger the King of all kings.

20.o: To engage in quarrels is the greatest folly that can be. Yield, and even give up just demands, for peace’ sake.
20.m: Fighting over disagreements is very foolish. Give in and even let go of what you deserve to keep the peace.

20.o: He who labours and endures hardship in his seed-time for eternity, will be properly diligent as to his earthly business.
20.m: He who works hard and faces difficulties during his time of planting will be careful about his daily work.

20.o: Though many capable of giving wise counsel are silent, yet something may be drawn from them, which will reward those who obtain it.
20.m: Even if many wise people stay quiet, there is still value in what they say if you listen to them.

20.o: It is hard to find those that have done, and will do more good than they speak, or care to hear spoken of.
20.m: It's hard to find people who do more good than they talk about or want others to know about.

20.o: A good man is not liable to uneasiness in contriving what he shall do, or in reflecting on what he has done, as those who walk in deceit. And his family fare better for his sake.
20.m: A good person doesn't worry about what they should do or feel bad about things they've done. They don't have the troubles that people who aren't honest have. Their family also benefits from their goodness.

20.o: If great men are good men, they may do much good, and prevent very much evil.
20.m: If important people are good, they can do a lot of good and stop a lot of bad things.

20.o: Some can say, Through grace, we are cleaner than we have been; but it was the work of the Holy Spirit.
20.m: Some people can say that through God's grace, they are now cleaner than before. But it was the Holy Spirit who did this work.

20.o: See the various deceits men use, of which the love of money is the root. The Lord will not bless what is thus gotten.
20.m: See all the tricks people use, and the love of money causes them. The Lord won't bless anything gained through those tricks.

20.o: Parents should observe their children, that they may manage them accordingly.
20.m: Parents should watch their children so they can raise them properly.

20.o: All our powers and faculties are from God, and are to be employed for him.
20.m: All our abilities come from God, and we should use them for him.

20.o: Those that indulge themselves, may expect to want necessaries, which should have been gotten by honest labour.
20.m: People who waste their time may find themselves lacking basic needs that they could have earned through hard work.

20.o: Men use arts to get a good bargain, and to buy cheap; whereas a man ought to be ashamed of a fraud and a lie.
20.m: People try to use tricks to get a good deal and buy things cheaply. But someone should feel embarrassed about being dishonest and lying.

20.o: He that prefers true knowledge to riches, follows the ways of religion and happiness. If we really believed this truth, the word of God would be valued as it deserves, and the world would lose its tempting influence.
20.m: If we choose real knowledge over money, we follow paths that lead to religious fulfillment and happiness. If we truly believe this, we would value God’s word as much as it should be valued, and the world’s temptations would have less power over us.

20.o: Those ruin themselves who entangle themselves in rash suretiship. Also those who are in league with abandoned women. Place no confidence in either.
20.m: People hurt themselves by promising to pay debts for others too quickly. They also get into trouble by associating with immoral women. Don't trust people in either of these situations.

20.o: Wealth gotten by fraud may be sweet, for the carnal mind takes pleasure in the success of wicked devices; but it will be bitter in the reflection.
20.m: Money gained through trickery might seem sweet at first because selfish people enjoy their evil plans working out. But later, it will taste bitter when you think about it.

20.o: Especially we need advice in spiritual warfare. The word and Spirit of God are the best counsellors in every point.
20.m: We especially need guidance in spiritual battles. The Word and Spirit of God are the best advisors for everything.

20.o: Those dearly buy their own praise, who put confidence in a man because he speaks fairly.
20.m: People waste their time getting praised by someone they trust just because that person talks nicely.

20.o: An undutiful child will become very miserable. Never let him expect any peace or comfort.
20.m: A child who doesn't listen won't be happy. They shouldn't expect any peace or comfort.

20.o: An estate suddenly raised, is often as suddenly ruined.
20.m: A fortune gained quickly is often lost just as quickly.

20.o: Wait on the Lord, attend his pleasure, and he will protect thee.
20.m: Wait for the Lord, do what pleases him, and he will protect you.

20.o: A bargain made by fraud will prove a losing bargain in the end.
20.m: A deal made through trickery will eventually be a bad deal.

20.o: How can we form plans, and conduct business, independently of the Lord?
20.m: How can we make plans and do business without relying on the Lord?

20.o: The evasions men often use with their own consciences show how false and deceitful man is.
20.m: The ways people try to avoid what their conscience tells them show how dishonest and deceptive people can be.

20.o: Justice should crush the wicked, and separate them from the virtuous.
20.m: Justice should punish the wicked and keep them apart from the good people.

20.o: The rational soul and conscience are as a lamp within us, which should be used in examining our dispositions and motives with the revealed will of God.
20.m: Our mind and conscience are like a light inside us. We should use this light to check if our thoughts and reasons match what God has told us.

20.o: Mercy and truth are the glories of God's throne.
20.m: God's throne is，。

20.o: Both young and old have their advantages; and let neither despise or envy the other.
20.m:
Young people and older people both have things they are good at.
Let neither group look down on or envy the other.

20.o: Severe rebukes sometimes do a great deal of good. But such is the corruption of nature, that men are loth to be rebuked for their sins. If God uses severe afflictions, to purify our hearts and fit us for his service, we have cause to be very thankful.
20.m: Sometimes harsh criticism can do a lot of good. But because people tend to resist being told about their mistakes, they often don't like getting criticized. When God uses tough situations to clean our hearts and prepare us for his work, we should be very grateful.

21.o: The believer, perceiving that the Lord rules every heart as he sees fit, like the husbandman who turns the water through his grounds as he pleases, seeks to have his own heart, and the hearts of others, directed in his faith, fear, and love.
21.m: The believer knows that the Lord controls everyone’s heart just like a farmer directs water over his fields. So, the believer wants their own heart and the hearts of others to be guided by faith, fear, and love.

21.o: We are partial in judging ourselves and our actions.
21.m: We tend to be unfair when we judge ourselves and what we do.

21.o: Many deceive themselves with a conceit that outward devotions will excuse unrighteousness.
21.m: Many people fool themselves into thinking that doing religious activities will excuse their wrong actions.

21.o: Sin is the pride, the ambition, the glory, the joy, and the business of wicked men.
21.m: Wicked people see sin as their pride, their ambition, their source of glory, their joy, and what they do for work.

21.o: The really diligent employ foresight as well as labour.
21.m: The truly hardworking people plan ahead as well as work hard.

21.o: While men seek wealth by unlawful practices, they seek death.
21.m: When people try to get rich through wrong methods, they are seeking death.

21.o: Injustice will return upon the sinner, and will destroy him here and for ever.
21.m: Wrongdoing will come back on the person who sins and will destroy them both now and forever.

21.o: The way of mankind by nature is froward and strange.
21.m: By nature, people tend to be stubborn and odd.

21.o: It is best to shun bitter contention by pouring out the heart before God. For by prudence and patience, with constant prayer, the cross may be removed.
21.m: It's better to avoid arguing by talking openly to God. With wisdom, patience, and constant praying, you can get rid of difficult situations.

21.o: The evil desires of a wicked man's heart, lead to baseness in his conduct.
21.m: A bad person's evil thoughts lead them to do mean things.

21.o: The simple may be made wise by punishments on the wicked, and by instructions to those who are willing to be taught.
21.m: Punishments on the wicked and teachings for those who want to learn can help simple people become wise.

21.o: Good men envy not the prosperity of evil-doers; they see there is a curse on them.
21.m: Good people do not envy those who do wrong when they prosper. They know that these wrongdoers are cursed.

21.o: Such as oppress the poor by beating down wages, such as will not relieve according to their ability those in distress, and those in authority who neglect to do justice, stop their ears at the cry of the poor. But doubtless care is to be used in the exercise of charity.
21.m: People who hurt the poor by paying them too little, those who don't help people in need as much as they should, and leaders who ignore doing what's right—these all close their ears to the cries of the poor. Still, we should use care when showing kindness.

21.o: If money can conquer the fury of the passions, shall reason, the fear of God, and the command of Christ, be too weak to bridle them?
21.m: If money can calm strong emotions, shouldn't reasoning, fearing God, and following Christ's commands be enough to control them?

21.o: There is true pleasure only in the practice of religion.
21.m: True happiness comes from following religious practices.

21.o: Of all wanderers in the ways of sin, those are in the most dangerous condition who turn aside into the ways of darkness. Yet there is hope even for them in the all-sufficient Saviour; but let them flee to him without delay.
21.m: People who go off course into sinful paths are in great danger. But even they can find help through the Savior. They should run to him right away.

21.o: A life of worldly pleasure brings ruin on men.
21.m: A life focused on worldly pleasures leads to destruction.

21.o: The righteous is often delivered out of trouble, and the wicked comes in his stead, and so seems as a ransom for him.
21.m: The righteous person is often saved from trouble, and the wicked takes their place, making it seem like the wicked is paying the price for them.

21.o: Unbridled passions spoil the comfort of all relations.
21.m: Uncontrolled emotions ruin the happiness in all relationships.

21.o: The plenty obtained by prudence, industry, and frugality, is desirable. But the foolish misspend what they have upon their lusts.
21.m: Being smart, hardworking, and careful with money brings plenty of good things. But foolish people waste what they have on their desires.

21.o: True repentance and faith will lead him that relies on the mercy of God in Christ, to follow after righteousness and mercy in his own conduct.
21.m: True repentance and faith will lead someone who depends on God’s mercy through Christ to act righteously and mercifully themselves.

21.o: Those that have wisdom, often do great things, even against those confident of their strength.
21.m: People who are wise often accomplish great things, even against those who are very confident in their own strength.

21.o: It is our great concern to keep our souls from being entangled and disquieted.
21.m: Our main worry should be keeping our minds peaceful and free from troubles.

21.o: Pride and haughtiness make men passionate; such continually deal in wrath, as if it were their trade to be angry.
21.m: Pride makes people get angry easily. They always seem upset, like anger is their job.

21.o: Here is the misery of the slothful; their hands refuse to labour in an honest calling, by which they might get an honest livelihood; yet their hearts cease not to covet riches, pleasures, and honours, which cannot be obtained without labour. But the righteous and industrious have their desires satisfied.
21.m: The lazy person faces trouble because they won't work hard for money. Yet, they keep wanting nice things that need effort to get. The honest workers who put in the effort get what they want.

21.o: When holiness is pretended, but wickedness intended, that especially is an abomination.
21.m: When someone pretends to be holy but intends to do wicked things, that is especially disgusting.

21.o: The doom of a false witness is certain.
21.m: The punishment for a false witness is sure.

21.o: A wicked man bids defiance to the terrors of the law and the rebukes of Providence. But a good man asks, What does God require of me?
21.m: A bad person ignores the warnings of the law and the lessons from life’s troubles. A good person wonders, What does God want me to do?

21.o: Means are to be used, but, after all, our safety and salvation are only of the Lord. In our spiritual warfare we must arm ourselves with the whole armour of God; but our strength must be in the Lord, and in the power of his might.
21.m: We should use the right methods, but ultimately, our safety and salvation come from the Lord. In our spiritual battles, we need to protect ourselves with the full armor of God. But our real strength comes from the Lord and his power.

22.o: We should be more careful to do that by which we may get and keep a good name, than to raise or add unto a great estate.
22.m: We should be more careful about doing things that help us have a good reputation than about building up a large fortune.

22.o: Divine Providence has so ordered it, that some are rich, and others poor, but all are guilty before God; and at the throne of God's grace the poor are as welcome as the rich.
22.m: God makes some people rich and others poor, but everyone is guilty before God. At God's throne of grace, poor people are just as welcome as rich people.

22.o: Faith foresees the evil coming upon sinners, and looks to Jesus Christ as the sure refuge from the storm.
22.m: Faith sees the trouble coming for those who sin and turns to Jesus Christ for safety.

22.o: Where the fear of God is, there will be humility. And much is to be enjoyed by it; spiritual riches, and eternal life at last.
22.m: When you fear God, you will be humble. Humility brings many blessings, including spiritual wealth and eternal life.

22.o: The way of sin is vexatious and dangerous. But the way of duty is safe and easy.
22.m:
Sin causes trouble and danger. 
Doing what is right is safe and easy.

22.o: Train children, not in the way they would go, that of their corrupt hearts, but in the way they should go; in which, if you love them, you would have them go. As soon as possible every child should be led to the knowledge of the Saviour.
22.m: Train children not in the ways of their sinful nature, but in the ways they should follow. If you love them, you want them to walk in those right paths. Teach them about the Savior as early as possible.

22.o: This shows how important it is for every man to keep out of debt. As to the things of this life, there is a difference between the rich and the poor; but let the poor remember, it is the Lord that made the difference.
22.m: This shows how important it is for everyone to avoid being in debt. There is a difference between the lives of the rich and the poor. But the poor should remember that God is the one who made that difference.

22.o: The power which many abuse, will soon fail them.
22.m: The power that many misuse will soon fail them.

22.o: He that seeks to relieve the wants and miseries of others shall be blessed.
22.m: Anyone who helps people in need will be blessed.

22.o: Profane scoffers and revilers disturb the peace.
22.m: People who are rude and mean disrupt the peace.

22.o: God will be the Friend of a man in whose spirit there is no guile; this honour have all the saints.
22.m: God will be the friend of someone who has no deceit in their heart. This honor belongs to all the saints.

22.o: God turns the counsels and designs of treacherous men to their own confusion.
22.m: God turns the plans of dishonest people against them.

22.o: The slothful man talks of a lion without, but considers not his real danger from the devil, that roaring lion within, and from his own slothfulness, which kills him.
22.m: The lazy person worries about a lion outside, but doesn't think about the real danger from the devil, the roaring lion inside. They also don't consider how their laziness harms them.

22.o: The vile sin of licentiousness commonly besots the mind beyond recovery.
22.m: The terrible sin of being too free with sexual immorality often clouds the mind so badly that it cannot be fixed.

22.o: Sin is foolishness, it is in the heart, there is an inward inclination to sin: children bring it into the world with them; and it cleaves close to the soul. We all need to be corrected by our heavenly Father.
22.m: Sin is foolish. It starts in the heart, where we have a tendency toward it. Children are born with this sinful nature. It sticks closely to the soul. We all need correction from our heavenly Father.

22.o: We are but stewards, and must distribute what God intrusts to our care, according to his will.
22.m: We are just caretakers and need to share what God gives us according to his wishes.

22.o: To these words, to this knowledge, the ear must be bowed down, and the heart applied by faith and love. To live a life of delight in God and dependence on him, is the foundation of all practical religion. The way to know the certainty of the word of truth, is to make conscience of our duty.
22.m: We need to listen carefully and apply what we hear by having faith and love. Living a life where we take joy in God and depend on him is the basis for true religion. To be sure about God’s word, we must do our duties faithfully.

22.o: He that robs and oppresses the poor, does so at his peril. And if men will not appear for them, God will.
22.m: The person who steals from and hurts the poor does so at their own risk. If people won't stand up for the poor, God will.

22.o: Our corrupt hearts have so much tinder in them, that it is dangerous to have to do with those that throw about the sparks of their passion.
22.m: Our sinful hearts are so full of things that can easily catch fire that it's dangerous to be around people who let their anger get out of control.

22.o: Every man ought to be just to himself, and his family; those are not so, who, by folly or other carelessness, waste what they have.
22.m: Everyone should be fair to themselves and their family. People who waste what they have through foolishness or carelessness are not being fair.

22.o: We are taught not to trespass on another man's right. And it is hard to find a truly industrious man. Such a man will rise. Seest thou a man diligent in the business of religion? He is likely to excel. Let us then be diligent in God's work.
22.m: We learn not to take what belongs to someone else. It’s rare to find a truly hardworking person. A hardworking person usually succeeds. Do you see someone who works hard at their religious duties? They often do very well. So let’s be diligent in doing God’s work.

23.o: God's restraints of the appetite only say, Do thyself no harm.
23.m: God's limits on our desires tell us not to hurt ourselves.

23.o: Be not of those that will be rich. The things of this world are not happiness and a portion for a soul; those that hold them ever so fast, cannot hold them always, cannot hold them long.
23.m: Don't be someone who wants to be rich. The things in this world do not bring true happiness or fulfillment. Those who try to keep their wealth tightly won’t be able to keep it forever or for very long.

23.o: Do not make thyself burdensome to any, especially those not sincere. When we are called by God to his feast, and to let our souls delight themselves, Isa 25:6; 55:2, we may safely partake of the Bread of life.
23.m: Don't be a burden to others, especially those who aren't genuine. When God calls us to His feast and invites us to enjoy ourselves, as in Isaiah 25:6 and 55:2, we can eat the Bread of life without worry.

23.o: It is our duty to take all fit occasions to speak of Divine things; but if what a wise man says will not be heard, let him hold his peace.
23.m: We should talk about God whenever we have a good chance. But if smart people aren't listening, it's best to stay quiet.

23.o: The fatherless are taken under God's special protection. He is their Redeemer, who will take their part; and he is mighty, almighty.
23.m: God protects children who have lost their fathers. He is their defender and has all power to help them.

23.o: Here is a parent instructing his child to give his mind to the Scriptures. Here is a parent correcting his child: accompanied with prayer, and blessed of God, it may prove a means of preventing his destruction. Here is a parent encouraging his child, telling him what would be for his good. And what a comfort it would be, if herein he answered his expectation!
23.m: Here is a parent teaching their child to focus on the Scriptures. The parent corrects their child while praying, hoping it will stop them from making mistakes. They encourage their child by explaining what’s best for them. It would be so nice if the child met these expectations.

23.o: The believer's expectation shall not be disappointed; the end of his trials, and of the sinner's prosperity, is at hand.
23.m: The believer's hopes will not be let down. The end of their trials and the end of the sinner's good fortune are near.

23.o: The gracious Saviour who purchased pardon and peace for his people, with all the affection of a tender parent, counsels us to hear and be wise, and is ready to guide our hearts in his way. Here we have an earnest call to young people, to attend to the advice of their godly parents. If the heart be guided, the steps will be guided. Buy the truth, and sell it not; be willing to part with any thing for it. Do not part with it for pleasures, honours, riches, or any thing in this world. The heart is what the great God requires. We must not think to divide the heart between God and the world; he will have all or none. Look to the rule of God's word, the conduct of his providence, and the good examples of his people. Particular cautions are given against sins most destructive to wisdom and grace in the soul. It is really a shame to make a god of the belly. Drunkenness stupifies men, and then all goes to ruin. Licentiousness takes away the heart that should be given to God. Take heed of any approaches toward this sin, it is very hard to retreat from it. It bewitches men to their ruin.
23.m:
The kind Savior who bought forgiveness and peace for his followers, with all the care of a loving parent, tells us to listen and learn wisely. He wants to help guide our hearts on the right path. 

This passage urges young people especially to pay attention to their godly parents' advice. When the heart is led well, the actions follow too. 

Buy truth and don't ever let it go. Be willing to give up anything you have to get it. Don’t trade it for fun, fame, money, or anything else in this world. 

God asks for all of our hearts. We cannot split our loyalty between him and worldly things—he wants everything or nothing at all. 

Follow God’s rules in the Bible, see how he guides you through life, and watch those who love him. 

There are warnings about certain sins that destroy wisdom and faith. Worshiping food and drink is shameful. Drinking too much makes people stupid and ruins them. Being sexually immoral steals the heart meant for God. Stay far away from these dangers because once you start, it’s hard to stop. This sin tricks people into self-destruction.

23.o: Solomon warns against drunkenness. Those that would be kept from sin, must keep from all the beginnings of it, and fear coming within reach of its allurements. Foresee the punishment, what it will at last end in, if repentance prevent not. It makes men quarrel. Drunkards wilfully make woe and sorrow for themselves. It makes men impure and insolent. The tongue grows unruly; the heart utters things contrary to reason, religion, and common civility. It stupifies and besots men. They are in danger of death, of damnation; as much exposed as if they slept upon the top of a mast, yet feel secure. They fear no peril when the terrors of the Lord are before them; they feel no pain when the judgments of God are actually upon them. So lost is a drunkard to virtue and honour, so wretchedly is his conscience seared, that he is not ashamed to say, I will seek it again. With good reason we were bid to stop before the beginning. Who that has common sense would contract a habit, or sell himself to a sin, which tends to such guilt and misery, and exposes a man every day to the danger of dying insensible, and awaking in hell? Wisdom seems in these chapters to take up the discourse as at the beginning of the book. They must be considered as the words of Christ to the sinner.
23.m:
Solomon warns against getting drunk. To avoid sin, you need to stay away from anything that might lead to it. Think about the consequences—what could happen if you don’t change your ways. Drinking too much leads to fights. Drunks often cause their own unhappiness and trouble. 

It makes people act badly. Their speech becomes unreasonable and disrespectful. Drinking also clouds their minds. They risk death and eternal punishment. They’re as dangerous as sleeping on the tip of a mast, yet they think nothing bad will happen.

They ignore the dangers ahead and don’t feel the pain of God’s judgment. A drunk loses all sense of right and wrong. He feels no shame saying, “I want more.” That’s why Solomon tells us to stop before we start. Why would anyone choose a habit that brings so much harm and puts them in danger of dying without knowing better?

In these chapters, wisdom speaks as if it was at the start of the book. These words come from Jesus speaking to those who have strayed.

24.o: Envy not sinners. And let not a desire ever come into thy mind, Oh that I could shake off restraints!
24.m:
Don't envy sinners. 

Never wish that you could break free from rules.

24.o: Piety and prudence in outward affairs, both go together to complete a wise man. By knowledge the soul is filled with the graces and comforts of the spirit, those precious and pleasant riches. The spirit is strengthened for the spiritual work and the spiritual warfare, by true wisdom.
24.m: Being good and smart about daily things makes a wise person complete. Knowledge fills the soul with spiritual graces and comforts, which are valuable and enjoyable. True wisdom strengthens the spirit for its tasks and battles.

24.o: A weak man thinks wisdom is too high for him, therefore he will take no pains for it. It is bad to do evil, but worse to devise it. Even the first risings of sin in the heart are sin, and must be repented of. Those that strive to make others hateful, make themselves so.
24.m: A weak person thinks wisdom is out of reach, so they don't try to get it. Doing evil is wrong, but planning evil is even worse. Even thinking about doing something sinful is sin, and you should feel sorry for it. People who try to make others hate them end up hated themselves.

24.o: Under troubles we are apt to despair of relief. But be of good courage, and God shall strengthen thy heart.
24.m: When you face problems, you might feel hopeless. But stay brave, and God will give you strength.

24.o: If a man know that his neighbour is in danger by any unjust proceeding, he is bound to do all in his power to deliver him. And what is it to suffer immortal souls to perish, when our persuasions and example may be the means of preventing it?
24.m: If someone knows their neighbor is in danger because of unfair treatment, they should try their best to help them. What is it to let immortal souls be lost when our words and actions could stop it?

24.o: We are quickened to the study of wisdom by considering both the pleasure and the profit of it. All men relish things that are sweet to the palate; but many have no relish for the things that are sweet to the purified soul, and that make us wise unto salvation.
24.m: We are encouraged to learn wisdom by thinking about both its enjoyment and its benefits. Everyone enjoys things that taste good, but many do not enjoy the things that bring spiritual joy and help us become wise for our salvation.

24.o: The sincere soul falls as a traveller may do, by stumbling at some stone in his path; but gets up, and goes on his way with more care and speed. This is rather to be understood of falls into affliction, than falls into actual sin.
24.m:
A honest person might stumble, like a traveler who trips over a stone. But they get back up and keep going with more caution and hurry. 

This usually means falling into trouble, not necessarily into sin.

24.o: The pleasure we are apt to take in the troubles of an enemy is forbidden.
24.m: We should not enjoy seeing our enemies have problems.

24.o: Envy not the wicked their prosperity; be sure there is no true happiness in it.
24.m: Don't envy the wicked for their success. There is no real happiness in it.

24.o: The godly in the land, will be quiet in the land. There may be cause to change for the better, but have nothing to do with them that are given change.
24.m: The godly people will remain peaceful. They might have reasons to improve their situation, but they won’t join those who seek change.

24.o: The wisdom God giveth, renders a man fit for his station. Every one who finds the benefit of the right answer, will be attached to him that gave it.
24.m: The wisdom that God gives makes a person suitable for their role. Anyone who sees the value of the correct answer will stay loyal to the one who provided it.

24.o: We must prefer necessaries before conveniences, and not go in debt.
24.m: We should choose necessities over conveniences and avoid going into debt.

24.o: There are three defaults in a witness pointed out.
24.m: There are three mistakes a witness can make.

24.o: See what a blessing the husbandman's calling is, and what a wilderness this earth would be without it. See what great difference there is in the management even of worldly affairs. Sloth and self-indulgence are the bane of all good. When we see fields overgrown with thorns and thistles, and the fences broken down, we see an emblem of the far more deplorable state of many souls. Every vile affection grows in men's hearts; yet they compose themselves to sleep. Let us show wisdom by doubling our diligence in every good thing.
24.m:
Farmers do such important work. Can you imagine how empty the world would be without them? 

There’s a big difference in how people handle their jobs. Laziness and selfishness ruin everything. 

When we see fields full of weeds and broken fences, it reminds us of how bad things get inside some people too. Bad feelings grow in their hearts, but they still try to sleep.

Let’s be smart and work hard at doing good things.

25.o: God needs not search into any thing; nothing can be hid from him. But it is the honour of rulers to search out matters, to bring to light hidden works of darkness.
25.m: God doesn't need to look for anything because he knows everything. But it's important for leaders to investigate things and uncover secrets.

25.o: For a prince to suppress vice, and reform his people, is the best way to support his government.
25.m: It's best for a leader to stop bad behavior and improve their people to keep the government strong.

25.o: Religion teaches us humility and self-denial. He who has seen the glory of the Lord in Christ Jesus, will feel his own unworthiness.
25.m: Religion teaches us to be humble and deny ourselves. When we see the glory of the Lord in Christ Jesus, we realize how unworthy we are.

25.o: To be hasty in beginning strife, will bring into difficulties. War must at length end, and might better be prevented. It is so in private quarrels; do all thou canst to settle the matter.
25.m: Being quick to start an argument leads to trouble. Wars eventually end, but it’s better to avoid them altogether. This is true for personal disputes too. Do what you can to resolve things peacefully.

25.o: A word of counsel, or reproof, rightly spoken, is especially beautiful, as fine fruit becomes still more beautiful in silver baskets.
25.m: Good advice or gentle correction, when given properly, is very beautiful, like how pretty fruit looks even better in a silver basket.

25.o: See what ought to be the aim of him that is trusted with any business; to be faithful. A faithful minister, Christ's messenger, should be thus acceptable to us.
25.m: We should aim to be faithful if we are given any task. A minister who serves Christ well should be someone we trust.

25.o: He who pretends to have received or given that which he never had, is like the morning cloud, that disappoints those who look for rain.
25.m: Someone who claims to have given or received something they never did is like a morning cloud that lets people down when they expect rain.

25.o: Be patient to bear a present hurt. Be mild to speak without passion; for persuasive language is the most effectual to prevail over the hardened mind.
25.m: Be patient if you are hurt now. Speak calmly without getting angry. Calm words work best to convince stubborn people.

25.o: God has given us leave to use grateful things, but we are cautioned against excess.
25.m: God allows us to enjoy good things, but he warns us not to be excessive.

25.o: We cannot be upon good terms with our neighbours, without discretion as well as sincerity. How much better a Friend is God than any other friend! The oftener we come to him, the more welcome.
25.m: We can't get along well with our neighbors unless we are both honest and careful. God is a much better friend than anyone else. The more often we talk to him, the more he welcomes us.

25.o: A false testimony is dangerous in every thing.
25.m: False testimony is dangerous in everything.

25.o: Confidence in an unfaithful man is painful and vexatious; when we put any stress on him, he not only fails, but makes us feel for it.
25.m: Relying on someone who isn't trustworthy causes pain and trouble. When you depend on them, they let you down and cause you distress.

25.o: We take a wrong course if we think to relieve those in sorrow by endeavouring to make them merry.
25.m: We go wrong if we try to help sad people by trying to cheer them up.

25.o: The precept to love even our enemies is an Old Testament commandment. Our Saviour has shown his own great example in loving us when we were enemies.
25.m: The command to love our enemies comes from the Old Testament. Jesus showed us how to do this by loving us when we were his enemies.

25.o: Slanders would not be so readily spoken, if they were not readily heard. Sin, if it receives any check, becomes cowardly.
25.m: People wouldn't spread rumors so easily if others didn't listen to them. Sins become less bold if they face any kind of resistance.

25.o: It is better to be alone, than to be joined to one who is a hinderance to the comfort of life.
25.m: It's better to be alone than to be with someone who makes your life less comfortable.

25.o: Heaven is a country afar off; how refreshing is good news from thence, in the everlasting gospel, which signifies glad tidings, and in the witness of the Spirit with our spirits that we are God's children!
25.m: Heaven feels far away. How wonderful it is to hear good news from there! The everlasting gospel brings happy messages, and the Spirit within us confirms that we are God's children.

25.o: When the righteous are led into sin, it is as hurtful as if the public fountains were poisoned.
25.m: When good people do wrong, it harms others just like poisoning a public water source would.

25.o: We must be, through grace, dead to the pleasures of sense, and also to the praises of men.
25.m: We need to be free from the desire for worldly pleasures and the praise of others, thanks to God's grace.

25.o: The man who has no command over his anger, is easily robbed of peace. Let us give up ourselves to the Lord, and pray him to put his Spirit within us, and cause us to walk in his statutes.
25.m: A person who can't control their anger loses peace easily. We should give ourselves to the Lord and ask him to fill us with his Spirit so we can follow his rules.

26.o: Honour is out of season to those unworthy and unfit for it.
26.m: Honor doesn't fit those who don't deserve it.

26.o: He that is cursed without cause, the curse shall do him no more harm than the bird that flies over his head.
26.m: A curse from someone who has no reason to curse you will not hurt you anymore than a bird flying overhead.

26.o: Every creature must be dealt with according to its nature, but careless and profligate sinners never will be ruled by reason and persuasion. Man indeed is born like the wild ass's colt; but some, by the grace of God, are changed.
26.m:
We should treat each creature based on its nature. But people who don't care about right and wrong won't listen to reason or persuasion. 

People start out like wild animals, but some are changed by God's grace.

26.o: We are to fit our remarks to the man, and address them to his conscience, so as may best end the debate.
26.m: We should tailor our comments to the person and speak directly to their conscience in a way that helps end the argument.

26.o: Fools are not fit to be trusted, nor to have any honour. Wise sayings, as a foolish man delivers and applies them, lose their usefulness.
26.m: Fools shouldn't be trusted or given honor. Wise sayings become useless when a fool shares them or tries to use them.

26.o: This verse may either declare how the Lord, the Creator of all men, will deal with sinners according to their guilt, or, how the powerful among men should disgrace and punish the wicked.
26.m: This verse could be talking about how God, who created everyone, will deal with people who do wrong based on what they have done. Or it could be saying that powerful people should shame and punish those who act badly.

26.o: The dog is a loathsome emblem of those sinners who return to their vices, 2Pe 2:22.
26.m: The dog is an unpleasant symbol for people who go back to their bad habits, 2 Peter 2:22.

26.o: We see many a one who has some little sense, but is proud of it. This describes those who think their spiritual state to be good, when really it is very bad.
26.m: We often see people who have just a little bit of understanding but are very proud of it. This applies to those who think their spiritual condition is good when it is actually very bad.

26.o: The slothful man hates every thing that requires care and labour. But it is foolish to frighten ourselves from real duties by fancied difficulties. This may be applied to a man slothful in the duties of religion.
26.m: A lazy person avoids anything that needs effort or work. It’s silly to avoid our true responsibilities because we imagine them as too hard. We can apply this to someone who avoids religious duties.

26.o: Having seen the slothful man in fear of his work, here we find him in love with his ease. Bodily ease is the sad occasion of many spiritual diseases. He does not care to get forward with his business. Slothful professors turn thus. The world and the flesh are hinges on which they are hung; and though they move in a course of outward services, yet they are not the nearer to heaven.
26.m: We saw the lazy person afraid of their work before. Now we see them loving comfort instead. Physical comfort often leads to spiritual problems. They don't want to do their tasks. Lazy believers act this way too. The world and their desires keep them going. Even if they serve God outwardly, they aren’t getting closer to heaven.

26.o: The sluggard is now out of his bed, but he might have lain there, for any thing he is likely to bring to pass in his work. It is common for men who will not do their duty, to pretend they cannot. Those that are slothful in religion, will not be at the pains to feed their souls with the bread of life, nor to fetch in promised blessings by prayer.
26.m: The lazy person is finally out of bed, but they could have stayed there because nothing much will get done anyway. People often say they can't do their duties when really they just won’t do them. Lazy people in religion don’t want to put in the effort to feed their souls with God’s word or pray for blessings.

26.o: He that takes pains in religion, knows he is working for a good Master, and that his labour shall not be in vain.
26.m: If you work hard in religion, remember you have a good master, and your effort will not be wasted.

26.o: To make ourselves busy in other men's matters, is to thrust ourselves into temptation.
26.m: Getting involved in other people's business puts us in trouble.

26.o: He that sins in jest, must repent in earnest, or his sin will be his ruin.
26.m: If you sin while joking around, you need to be serious about repenting, or your sin could destroy you.

26.o: Contention heats the spirit, and puts families and societies into a flame. And that fire is commonly kindled and kept burning by whisperers and backbiters.
26.m:
Arguments heat people up and cause families and communities to become angry.
This anger often starts and continues because of gossip and people talking badly about others behind their backs.

26.o: A wicked heart disguising itself, is like a potsherd covered with the dross of silver.
26.m: A bad heart trying to hide is like a broken piece of pottery coated with silver trash.

26.o: Always distrust when a man speaks fair unless you know him well. Satan, in his temptations, speaks fair, as he did to Eve; but it is madness to give credit to him.
26.m: Always be careful when someone seems too nice, especially if you don't know them well. Satan uses sweet talk to trick people, just like he did with Eve. It's foolish to believe him.

26.o: What pains men take to do mischief to others! but it is digging a pit, it is rolling a stone, hard work; and they prepare mischief to themselves.
