26.m: People go to great lengths to harm others, but it’s like digging a hole or rolling a heavy rock—hard work. In the end, they only hurt themselves.

26.o: There are two sorts of lies equally detestable. A slandering lie, the mischief of this every body sees. A flattering lie, which secretly works ruin. A wise man will be more afraid of a flatterer than of a slanderer.
26.m: There are two kinds of lies that are both very bad. One is telling mean things about someone that aren't true. Everyone can see how harmful this is. The other is saying nice things that aren't true. This kind of lie can cause harm without anyone noticing. A smart person is more scared of someone who flatters them than of someone who speaks badly about them.

27.o: We know not what a day may bring forth. This does not forbid preparing for to-morrow, but presuming upon to-morrow. We must not put off the great work of conversion, that one thing needful.
27.m: We don't know what will happen tomorrow. This doesn't stop us from planning ahead, but it does mean we shouldn't count on having another day. We should not delay getting ready for God, the most important task.

27.o: There may be occasion for us to justify ourselves, but not to praise ourselves.
27.m:
We might need to defend ourselves, but we shouldn’t。 

(Note: It seems there was a mix-up with the translation. Here's the corrected modernized version in proper English):

We might need to defend ourselves, but we shouldn’t praise ourselves.

27.o: Those who have no command of their passions, sink under the load.
27.m: People who cannot control their emotions struggle under life’s burdens.

27.o: Plain and faithful rebukes are better, not only than secret hatred, but than love which compliments in sin, to the hurt of the soul.
27.m: Straightforward and honest criticism is better than hidden resentment. It is also better than false praise that hurts someone by encouraging them in their sins.

27.o: The poor have a better relish of their enjoyments, and are often more thankful for them, than the rich. In like manner the proud and self-sufficient disdain the gospel; but those who hunger and thirst after righteousness, find comfort from the meanest book or sermon that testifies of Christ Jesus.
27.m: The poor often enjoy what they have more than the rich do, and they are usually more grateful for it. Similarly, proud people who think they don’t need anything look down on the gospel. But those who really want to be righteous feel happy even from simple books or sermons that talk about Christ Jesus.

27.o: Every man has his proper place in society, where he may be safe and comfortable.
27.m: Everyone has a place in society where they can be safe and comfortable.

27.o: Depend not for relief upon a kinsman, merely for kindred's sake; apply to those who are at hand, and will help in need. But there is a Friend that sticketh closer than a brother, and let us place entire confidence in him.
27.m:
Don’t depend on a relative just because you’re related. Instead, turn to those who are nearby and willing to help.

But there is a friend who is even closer than a brother. Put all your trust in that friend.

27.o: An affectionate parent urges his son to prudent conduct that should gladden his heart. The good conduct of Christians is the best answer to all who find fault with the gospel.
27.m: A loving parent encourages their child to act wisely, which brings joy to their heart. The good behavior of Christians is the best response to those who criticize the gospel.

27.o: Where there is temptation, if we thrust ourselves into it, there will be sin, and punishment will follow.
27.m: When there is temptation, if we put ourselves in its way, sin will happen, and there will be consequences.

27.o: An honest man may be made a beggar, but he is not honest that makes himself one.
27.m: A honest person might become poor, but someone who chooses to be poor is not being honest.

27.o: It is folly to be fond of being praised; it is a temptation to pride.
27.m: It's silly to love getting praise. It can lead to being proud.

27.o: The contentions of a neighbour may be like a sharp shower, troublesome for a time; the contentions of a wife are like constant rain.
27.m: Arguments with a neighbor can be like a brief but annoying rainstorm. But arguments with a spouse can feel like endless rain.

27.o: We are cautioned to take heed whom we converse with. And directed to have in view, in conversation, to make one another wiser and better.
27.m: We should be careful about who we talk to. We should aim to help each other become smarter and better through our conversations.

27.o: Though a calling be laborious and despised, yet those who keep to it, will find there is something to be got by it. God is a Master who has engaged to honour those who serve him faithfully.
27.m: Even if a job is hard and not respected, people who stick with it will see benefits. God promises to honor those who serve him faithfully.

27.o: One corrupt heart is like another; so are sanctified hearts: the former bear the same image of the earthly, the latter the same image of the heavenly. Let us carefully watch our own hearts, comparing them with the word of God.
27.m: A corrupt heart is like any other corrupt heart. The same goes for holy hearts—they all reflect the same image, either of what’s earthly or what’s heavenly. We should closely check our own hearts by comparing them to God’s word.

27.o: Two things are here said to be never satisfied, death and sin. The appetites of the carnal mind for profit or pleasure are always desiring more. Those whose eyes are ever toward the Lord, are satisfied in him, and shall for ever be so.
27.m: Two things are never satisfied: death and sin. People who want money or pleasure always desire more. But those who look to the Lord are satisfied by him forever.

27.o: Silver and gold are tried by putting them into the furnace and fining-pot; so is a man tried by praising him.
27.m: Silver and gold are tested by putting them in a furnace and a refining pot. People are tested by seeing how they handle praise.

27.o: Some are so bad, that even severe methods do not answer the end; what remains but that they should be rejected? The new-creating power of God's grace alone is able to make a change.
27.m:
Some people are so bad that even strict measures don't help. What else can we do except reject them?
Only God's grace has the power to change someone for good.

27.o: We ought to have some business to do in this world, and not to live in idleness, and not to meddle with what we do not understand. We must be diligent and take pains. Let us do what we can, still the world cannot be secured to us, therefore we must choose a more lasting portion; but by the blessing of God upon our honest labours, we may expect to enjoy as much of earthly blessings as is good for us.
27.m:
We should have work to do in this world and avoid laziness. We shouldn't get involved in things we don't understand. We need to be hardworking and put in effort.

Let’s do what we can, but remember the world isn’t secure for us. So, we should pick something better that lasts longer. But if we work honestly, God will bless us with enough of the good things in life.

28.o: Sin makes men cowards. Whatever difficulties the righteous meet in the way of duty, they are not daunted.
28.m: Sin makes people afraid. Righteous people keep going even when they face problems while doing what is right.

28.o: National sins disturb the public repose.
28.m: National sins disrupt the peace of the whole country.

28.o: If needy persons get opportunities of oppressing, their extortion will be more severe than that of the more wealthy.
28.m: If poor people have chances to cheat others, they might be even meaner than richer people who do the same thing.

28.o: Wicked people strengthen one another in wicked ways.
28.m: Bad people encourage each other to do bad things.

28.o: If a man seeks the Lord, it is a good sign that he understands much, and it is a good means of understanding more.
28.m: If someone looks for the Lord, it shows they already know a lot, and it helps them learn even more.

28.o: An honest, godly, poor man, is better than a wicked, ungodly, rich man; has more comfort in himself, and is a greater blessing to the world.
28.m: A honest and godly person who is poor is better than someone who is wicked and ungodly but rich. The good person has more happiness and is more of a blessing to others.

28.o: Companions of riotous men not only grieve their parents, but shame them.
28.m: Friends who act badly not only upset their parents but also bring shame on them.

28.o: That which is ill got, though it may increase much, will not last long. Thus the poor are repaid, and God is glorified.
28.m: Money gotten dishonestly may grow larger, but it won’t last long. This shows what happens to those who act poorly, and it honors God.

28.o: The sinner at whose prayers God is angry, is one who obstinately refuses to obey God's commands.
28.m: God gets angry with someone whose prayers don't matter because they refuse to follow his rules.

28.o: The success of ungodly men is their own misery.
28.m: The success of bad people brings them misery.

28.o: Rich men are so flattered, that they think themselves superior to others.
28.m: Rich people are often praised so much that they think they are better than others.

28.o: There is glory in the land when the righteous have liberty.
28.m: The land prospers when righteous people are free.

28.o: It is folly to indulge sin, and excuse it. He who covers his sins, shall not have any true peace. He who humbly confesses his sins, with true repentance and faith, shall find mercy from God. The Son of God is our great atonement. Under a deep sense of our guilt and danger, we may claim salvation from that mercy which reigns through righteousness unto eternal life, by Jesus Christ our Lord.
28.m:
It’s foolish to hide our mistakes and make excuses for them. Someone who tries to cover up their sins won’t have real peace. But if you admit your sins honestly and truly feel sorry, you will receive mercy from God. 

The Son of God came to pay for our sins. When we deeply realize how guilty we are and how much danger we’re in, we can ask for the mercy that leads to eternal life through Jesus Christ.

28.o: There is a fear which causes happiness. Faith and love will deliver from the fear of eternal misery; but we should always fear offending God, and fear sinning against him.
28.m: There is a kind of fear that brings happiness. Faith and love protect us from the fear of eternal punishment. But we should always be afraid of disappointing God and of sinning against him.

28.o: A wicked ruler, whatever we may call him, this scripture calls a roaring lion, and a ranging bear.
28.m: A bad leader, no matter what we call them, this scripture describes as a roaring lion or a wandering bear.

28.o: Oppressors want understanding; they do not consult their own honour, ease, and safety.
28.m: People who oppress others don't seek wisdom. They don't think about what would be best for them, such as being respected, comfortable, or safe.

28.o: The murderer shall be haunted with terrors. None shall desire to save him from deserved punishment, nor pity him.
28.m: The murderer will be tormented by fears. Nobody will want to save them from their rightful punishment, nor feel sorry for them.

28.o: Uprightness will give men holy security in the worst times; but the false and dishonest are never safe.
28.m: Being honest gives people peace of mind even in difficult times, but those who are deceitful are always unsafe.

28.o: Those who are diligent, take the way to live comfortably.
28.m: People who work hard find comfort in their lives.

28.o: The true way to be happy, is to be holy and honest; not to raise an estate suddenly, without regard to right or wrong.
28.m: The real way to be happy is to be holy and honest. Don't try to get rich quickly by ignoring what's right or wrong.

28.o: Judgment is perverted, when any thing but pure right is considered.
28.m: Judgment is unfair when anything other than what is purely right is considered.

28.o: He that hastens to be rich, never seriously thinks how quickly God may take his wealth from him, and leave him in poverty.
28.m: Someone who rushes to get rich doesn't think about how fast God might take away their money and leave them poor.

28.o: Upon reflection, most will have a better opinion of a faithful reprover than of a soothing flatterer.
28.m: Most people will think more highly of someone who gives honest feedback than of someone who only says nice things.

28.o: Here is the wickedness of those who think it no sin to rob their parents, by wheedling them or threatening them, or by wasting what they have, and running into debt.
28.m: Here we see the wrongdoings of people who believe it's okay to cheat or bully their parents out of money, or spend all their parents' savings and get into debt.

28.o: Those make themselves always easy, that live in continual dependence upon God and his grace, and live by faith.
28.m: People stay relaxed when they depend on God all the time and trust in his help.

28.o: A fool trusts to his own strength, merit, and righteousness. And trusts to his own heart, which is not only deceitful above all things, but which has often deceived him.
28.m: A fool relies on their own strength, merit, and righteousness. They also trust their own heart, which is not only very deceitful but has often tricked them before.

28.o: A selfish man not only will not look out for objects of compassion, but will look off from those that call for his attention.
28.m: A self-centered person doesn't just avoid helping those who need it, but also ignores people who need their attention.

28.o: When power is put into the hands of the wicked, wise men decline public business. If the reader will go diligently over this and the other chapters, in many places where at first he may suppose there is least of Christ, still he will find what will lead to him.
28.m: When power goes to bad people, good leaders stay away from public work. If you look closely at this chapter and others, even where it seems like there is little about Christ, you will still find things that lead to him.

29.o: If God wounds, who can heal? The word of God warns all to flee from the wrath to come, to the hope set before us in Jesus Christ.
29.m:
If God hurts, who can fix it?
The Bible tells everyone to run away from God’s anger and turn to the hope we have in Jesus Christ.

29.o: The people have cause to rejoice or mourn, as their rulers are righteous or wicked.
29.m: People should be happy or sad depending on whether their leaders are good or bad.

29.o: Divine wisdom best keeps us from ruinous lusts.
29.m: God's wisdom helps keep us safe from harmful desires.

29.o: The Lord Jesus is the King who will minister true judgment to the people.
29.m: Jesus is the King who will fairly judge the people.

29.o: Flatterers put men off their guard, which betrays them into foolish conduct.
29.m: Flatterers make people let down their guard, which leads them to act foolishly.

29.o: Transgressions always end in vexations. Righteous men walk at liberty, and walk in safety.
29.m: Sin always leads to trouble. Good people live freely and safely.

29.o: This verse is applicable to compassion for the distress of the poor, and the unfeeling disregard shown by the wicked.
29.m: This verse talks about feeling sorry for people who are poor and suffering, while some bad people don't care at all.

29.o: The scornful mock at things sacred and serious. Men who promote religion, which is true wisdom, turn away the wrath of God.
29.m: People who mock holy things show their disrespect. Those who spread real wisdom—religion—can turn away God’s anger.

29.o: If a wise man dispute with a conceited wrangler, he will be treated with anger or ridicule; and no good is done.
29.m: If a wise person argues with someone who is proud and argumentative, that person might get angry or laugh at them. Nothing good comes from it.

29.o: Christ told his disciples that they should be hated of all men. The just, whom the blood-thirsty hate, gladly do any thing for their salvation.
29.m: Christ told his followers that they would be hated by everyone. The good people, who the violent hate, will do anything to save them.

29.o: He is a fool who tells every thing he knows, and can keep no counsel.
29.m: A fool shares everything he knows and cannot keep secrets.

29.o: One who loves flatterers, and hearkens to slanderers, causes his servants to become liars and false accusers.
29.m: A person who likes people who praise them too much and listens to those who spread gossip makes their servants lie and accuse others wrongly.

29.o: Some are poor, others have a great deal of deceitful riches. They meet in the business of this world; the Lord gives to both the comforts of this life. To some of both sorts he gives his grace.
29.m: Some people are poor, while others have lots of money that isn't really valuable. These people meet in their daily work. The Lord provides comfort to everyone, whether they are rich or poor. He also gives his grace to some of them, regardless of their wealth.

29.o: The rich will look to themselves, but the poor and needy the prince must defend and plead for.
29.m: The rich will take care of themselves, but the prince must protect and speak up for the poor and needy.

29.o: Parents must consider the benefit of due correction, and the mischief of undue indulgence.
29.m: Parents should think about the good of proper discipline and the harm of being too lenient.

29.o: Let not the righteous have their faith and hope shocked by the increase of sin and sinners, but let them wait with patience.
29.m: Don't let the faithful be shaken by seeing more sin and sinners around them. They should wait patiently.

29.o: Children must not be suffered to go without rebuke when they do amiss.
29.m: Children should not be allowed to go unpunished when they misbehave.

29.o: How bare does a place look without Bibles and ministers! and what an easy prey is it to the enemy of souls! That gospel is an open vision, which holds forth Christ, which humbles the sinner and exalts the Saviour, which promotes holiness in the life and conversation: and these are precious truths to keep the soul alive, and prevent it from perishing.
29.m: A place looks empty without Bibles and ministers. It becomes easy for evil to take over. The gospel shows us Jesus, makes us see our sins, and lifts up the Savior. It helps us live holy lives. These truths help our souls stay alive and stop them from dying.

29.o: Here is an unprofitable, slothful, wicked servant; one that serves not from conscience, or love, but from fear.
29.m: Here is a lazy, bad servant who doesn’t serve out of love or because he feels he should. He only serves out of fear.

29.o: When a man is self-conceited, rash, and given to wrangling, there is more hope of the ignorant and profligate.
29.m: When a person is proud, quick to act without thinking, and likes to argue, there is more hope for someone who is uneducated and behaves badly.

29.o: Good usage to a servant does not mean indulgence, which would ruin even a child. The body is a servant to the soul; those that humour it, and are over-tender of it, will find it forget its place.
29.m: Treating a servant well does not mean spoiling them, which could harm even a child. Our bodies serve our souls. People who pamper their bodies too much will find that their bodies forget their proper role.

29.o: An angry, passionate disposition makes men provoking to one another, and provoking to God.
29.m: An angry and passionate attitude makes people annoying to each other and to God.

29.o: Only those who humble themselves shall be exalted and established.
29.m: Only those who humbly lower themselves will be raised up and made strong.

29.o: The receiver is as bad as the thief.
29.m: The person who receives stolen goods is just as guilty as the thief.

29.o: Many are ashamed to own Christ now; and he will not own them in the day of judgment. But he that trusts in the Lord will be saved from this snare.
29.m: Many people are too embarrassed to admit they follow Christ now. So, he won't acknowledge them on Judgment Day. But those who trust in the Lord will be protected from this trap.

29.o: The wisest course is, to look to God, and seek the favour of the Ruler of rulers; for every creature is that to us which God makes it to be.
29.m: The best thing to do is to turn to God and ask for his favor, because he is the ruler over all rulers. Whatever happens, God decides what each creature means for us.

29.o: The just man abhors the sins of the wicked, and shuns their company. Christ exposed the wickedness of men, yet prayed for the wicked when they were crucifying him. Hatred to sin in ourselves and others, is a needful branch of the Christian temper. But all that are unholy, have rooted hatred to godliness.
29.m: A good person hates the sins of bad people and avoids being around them. Jesus pointed out the wrongdoings of people, but he still prayed for those who were hurting him on the cross. Disliking sin in ourselves and others is an important part of being a Christian. However, anyone who isn’t holy has deep dislike for things that are godly.

30.o: Agur speaks of himself as wanting a righteousness, and having done very foolishly. And it becomes us all to have low thoughts of ourselves. He speaks of himself as wanting revelation to guide him in the ways of truth and wisdom. The more enlightened people are, the more they lament their ignorance; the more they pray for clearer, still clearer discoveries of God, and his rich grace in Christ Jesus. In ver.#(4), there is a prophetic notice of Him who came down from heaven to be our Instructor and Saviour, and then ascended into heaven to be our Advocate. The Messiah is here spoken of as a Person distinct from the Father, but his name as yet secret. The great Redeemer, in the glories of his providence and grace, cannot be found out to perfection. Had it not been for Christ, the foundations of the earth had sunk under the load of the curse upon the ground, for man's sin. Who, and what is the mighty One that doeth all this? There is not the least ground to suspect anything wanting in the word of God; adding to his words opens the way to errors and corruptions.
30.m:
Agur talks about needing righteousness and doing foolish things. It’s good for everyone to think poorly of themselves. 

He says he needs guidance to know the right path. The smarter we get, the more we wish we knew even more. We should ask God to show us more clearly how kind and gracious he is through Jesus.

In verse four, Agur mentions someone coming down from heaven to teach and save us, and then going back up to heaven to help us. This person is the Messiah, different from God the Father, though his name isn’t known yet. 

The great Savior can't be fully understood because of his greatness and kindness. Without Christ, the Earth would have fallen apart due to the curse on it because of human sin. 

We shouldn’t doubt anything in God’s word. Adding to it just leads to mistakes and wrong ideas.

30.o: Agur wisely prayed for a middle state, that he might be kept at a distance from temptations; he asked daily bread suited to his station, his family, and his real good. There is a remarkable similarity between this prayer and several clauses of the Lord's prayer. If we are removed from vanity and lies; if we are interested in the pardoning love of Christ, and have him for our portion; if we walk with God, then we shall have all we can ask or think, as to spiritual things. When we consider how those who have abundance are prone to abuse the gift, and what it is to suffer want, Agur's prayer will ever be found a wise one, though seldom offered. Food convenient; what is so for one, may not be so for another; but we may be sure that our heavenly Father will supply all our need, and not suffer us to want anything good for us; and why should we wish for more?
30.m:
Agur wisely prayed for a balanced life where he would avoid temptation. He asked for enough food each day to suit his needs, his family’s needs, and what was truly best for him. His prayer has similarities to parts of the Lord's Prayer. 

If we stay away from pride and lies, if we receive forgiveness through Jesus and find joy in him, if we walk closely with God, then we will have everything we need spiritually. 

When we see how people with lots of money often misuse their wealth, and how hard it is to live without enough, we realize that Agur’s request makes sense. It’s smart to ask for just enough food—what suits one person might not suit another. But we can trust that our heavenly Father will provide everything we really need and nothing harmful. Why would we want more than that?

30.o: Slander not a servant to his master, accuse him not in small matters, to make mischief.
30.m: Don't spread lies about a servant to their boss. Don't complain about little things to cause trouble.

30.o: In every age there are monsters of ingratitude who ill-treat their parents. Many persuade themselves they are holy persons, whose hearts are full of sin, and who practise secret wickedness. There are others whose lofty pride is manifest. There have also been cruel monsters in every age.
30.m: In every age, there are people who are very ungrateful and mistreat their parents. Some think they are holy, but their hearts are full of sin and they do evil things secretly. Others are openly proud. And there have always been cruel people too.

30.o: Cruelty and covetousness are two daughters of the horseleech, that still cry, “Give, give,” and they are continually uneasy to themselves. Four things never are satisfied, to which these devourers are compared. Those are never rich that are always coveting. And many who have come to a bad end, have owned that their wicked courses began by despising their parents' authority.
30.m:
Cruelty and greed are like two daughters of a horseleech. They keep saying, "Give me more, give me more." They are always unhappy until they get what they want.

There are four things that are never satisfied. These cruel people are compared to them. People who are never happy with what they have will never be rich. Many people who ended up doing wrong said their bad behavior started because they didn't respect their parents' rules.

30.o: Four things cannot be fully known. The kingdom of nature is full of marvels. The fourth is a mystery of iniquity; the cursed arts by which a vile seducer gains the affections of a female; and the arts which a vile woman uses to conceal her wickedness.
30.m: There are four things that cannot be fully understood. One of them is the mysteries of evil actions. Another is how bad people use tricks to win someone’s affection. And there are also the ways that bad women hide their wrongdoings.

30.o: Four sorts of persons are very troublesome. Men of low origin and base spirit, who, getting authority, become tyrants. Foolish and violent men indulging in excesses. A woman of a contentious spirit and vicious habits. A servant who has obtained undue influence. Let those whom Providence has advanced from low beginnings, carefully watch against that sin which most easily besets them.
30.m:
Four types of people cause trouble. 

First, there are those who come from humble backgrounds and have mean spirits. When they gain power, they often turn into tyrants.

Second, there are foolish and violent people who let themselves go wild.

Third, there are women with quarrelsome natures and bad habits.

Fourth, there are servants who get too much control over others.

Those whom God has raised up from humble origins should be especially careful about the sins that。：

Those whom God has raised up from humble origins should be especially careful about the sins that。

，。

30.o: Four things that are little, are yet to be admired. There are those who are poor in the world, and of small account, yet wise for their souls and another world.
30.m: There are four small things that still deserve admiration. Some people have little money and aren't important in this world, but they are wise about their souls and the next life.

30.o: We may learn from animals to go well; also to keep our temper under all provocations. We must keep the evil thought in our minds from breaking out into evil speeches. We must not stir up the passions of others. Let nothing be said or done with violence, but every thing with softness and calmness. Alas, how often have we done foolishly in rising up against the Lord our King! Let us humble ourselves before him. And having found peace with Him, let us follow peace with all men.
30.m:
We can learn from animals to behave well and stay calm even when provoked. We need to stop bad thoughts from turning into mean words. We shouldn’t upset other people’s feelings. Everything should be said gently and calmly. 

How many times have we acted foolishly by going against God, who is our king? Let’s show humility and ask for forgiveness from him. Once we find peace with God, let’s try to get along peacefully with everyone else too.

31.o: When children are under the mother's eye, she has an opportunity of fashioning their minds aright. Those who are grown up, should often call to mind the good teaching they received when children. The many awful instances of promising characters who have been ruined by vile women, and love of wine, should warn every one to avoid these evils. Wine is to be used for want or medicine. Every creature of God is good, and wine, though abused, has its use. By the same rule, due praise and consolation should be used as cordials to the dejected and tempted, not administered to the confident and self-sufficient. All in authority should be more carefully temperate even than other men; and should be protectors of those who are unable or afraid to plead their own cause. Our blessed Lord did not decline the bitterest dregs of the cup of sorrow put into his hands; but he puts the cup of consolation into the hands of his people, and causes those to rejoice who are in the deepest distress.
31.m: When kids are watched closely by their mom, she can shape their minds well. Grown-ups should remember the good lessons they learned as children. Many young people with bright futures have been destroyed by bad women and drinking too much wine. This should warn everyone to stay away from such things. Wine can be helpful if someone needs it or if it’s used as medicine. Everything God made is good, and even wine, which can be misused, has its purpose. In the same way, we should give praise and comfort to those who feel down, not to those who think they don’t need help. People in charge should be extra careful about staying sober. They should also defend those who cannot speak up for themselves. Jesus didn't shy away from suffering. Instead, he gives his followers comfort and joy, even when they’re in deep trouble.

31.o: This is the description of a virtuous woman of those days, but the general outlines equally suit every age and nation. She is very careful to recommend herself to her husband's esteem and affection, to know his mind, and is willing that he rule over her. 1. She can be trusted, and he will leave such a wife to manage for him. He is happy in her. And she makes it her constant business to do him good. 2. She is one that takes pains in her duties, and takes pleasure in them. She is careful to fill up time, that none be lost. She rises early. She applies herself to the business proper for her, to women's business. She does what she does, with all her power, and trifles not. 3. She makes what she does turn to good account by prudent management. Many undo themselves by buying, without considering whether they can afford it. She provides well for her house. She lays up for hereafter. 4. She looks well to the ways of her household, that she may oblige all to do their duty to God and one another, as well as to her. 5. She is intent upon giving as upon getting, and does it freely and cheerfully. 6. She is discreet and obliging; every word she says, shows she governs herself by the rules of wisdom. She not only takes prudent measures herself, but gives prudent advice to others. The law of love and kindness is written in the heart, and shows itself in the tongue. Her heart is full of another world, even when her hands are most busy about this world. 7. Above all, she fears the Lord. Beauty recommends none to God, nor is it any proof of wisdom and goodness, but it has deceived many a man who made his choice of a wife by it. But the fear of God reigning in the heart, is the beauty of the soul; it lasts for ever. 8. She has firmness to bear up under crosses and disappointments. She shall reflect with comfort when she comes to be old, that she was not idle or useless when young. She shall rejoice in a world to come. She is a great blessing to her relations. If the fruit be good, the tree must have our good word. But she leaves it to her own works to praise her. Every one ought to desire this honour that cometh from God; and according to this standard we all ought to regulate our judgments. This description let all women daily study, who desire to be truly beloved and respected, useful and honourable. This passage is to be applied to individuals, but may it not also be applied to the church of God, which is described as a virtuous spouse? God by his grace has formed from among sinful men a church of true believers, to possess all the excellences here described.
31.m:
This describes a virtuous woman from long ago, but her qualities fit anyone at any time. 

First, she works hard to earn her husband’s respect and love. She knows what he likes and lets him lead. He is lucky to have her. She always tries to help him.

Second, she puts effort into doing her tasks and enjoys them too. She uses her time wisely so nothing is wasted. She wakes up early. She focuses on jobs suitable for women and does everything with all her strength.

Third, she manages things well so they benefit everyone. Some people spend money carelessly, but she buys carefully. She plans ahead and saves for the future.

Fourth, she watches over her home and family. She ensures everyone follows God’s commands and treats each other kindly.

Fifth, she cares just as much about giving as taking. She shares generously and happily.

Sixth, she speaks wisely and kindly. Each word reflects her inner wisdom. Not only does she act prudently, but she advises others wisely too. Kindness flows naturally from her heart through her words.

Seventh, she fears God above all else. Looks alone don’t impress God. True beauty comes from fearing God, which endures forever.

Eighth, she stays strong during tough times. When she grows older, she can look back proudly knowing she wasn't lazy. She finds joy in preparing for eternal life. She blesses her family now and forever. Good actions show a good character.

But she lets her good deeds speak for her. Everyone should want the honor that comes from God. We should use this as a guide for judging ourselves.

All women who want to be loved, respected, useful, and honored should study this description every day. 

This passage talks about individual people, but it could also apply to the church of God, which is compared to a virtuous wife. God uses his grace to form a group of true believers out of sinful people, giving them all these wonderful qualities.
